- Boat Life vs House Ownership — The Brutal Reality You Must See: Kind: captions Language: en Scrolling through dreamlike images of boat life, it is easy to believe swapping a house for a sailboat means instant freedom and lower bills. But what if the brutal reality is far less romantic? Most liabs juggle 150 ft of space, $700 a month in mortgage, and relentless upkeep, while homeowners get three times the space but pay a heavier mortgage. So, which is truly the better deal? And what hidden costs can sink your plans before you even cast off? A liboard boat rarely offers more than 150 to 400 square ft of interior space, sometimes less than a studio apartment. For many, that means a main cabin barely wide enough for two people to pass each other sideways, bunks pressed against whole walls, and a narrow galley where every meal feels like a puzzle. By comparison, the average American house stretches to about 2,000 square ft with separate bedrooms, full-sized appliances, closets, and storage for hobbies or seasonal gear. On a boat, every item has to earn its keep, and clutter piles up quickly. There is no attic or garage to hide extra tools, no basement for boxes of keepsakes. Even groceries become an exercise in logistics. Hauling bags down a swaying dock, then finding creative ways to fit a week's worth of food into a fridge the size of a hotel mini bar. Simple comforts like standing upright everywhere, stretching out on a sofa, or closing a door for privacy become rare luxuries. Dampness is a constant companion, especially in colder climates where condensation forms on every surface, and keeping bedding dry can turn into a daily battle. For families or couples, privacy often means ducking behind a thin curtain or stepping outside, weather permitting. The reality of boat life is learning to adapt to tight quarters, to embrace minimalism, not as a trend, but as a necessity. This physical constraint shapes every aspect of daily living. And over time, the lack of space can amplify the stress of routine chores and ongoing maintenance. A used liveboard sailboat often changes hands for $50,000 to $100,000, a fraction of what it takes to buy a house in most US cities. The average single family home now runs between $300,000 and $500,000 with down payments and closing costs stacking up before the first night's sleep. For many, the low sticker price of a boat is what draws them in, promising waterfront living at a cost that seems almost too good to be true. But the financial reality starts to shift the moment the keys are handed over. Monthly outflows on a boat and a house look different on paper, but both demand steady cash. A marina slip for a 35- ft sailboat can run anywhere from $700 to $1,200 a month, depending on location and amenities. That covers the right to tie up, basic utilities, and sometimes little more. Insurance for a boat adds another $100 to $300 a month. And even the most frugal liabs face fees for pumpouts, shore power, and occasional hallouts. Compare that to a typical mortgage payment of $1,500 to $2,500 a month, plus property taxes, insurance, and utilities. And the gap narrows fast. A financial planner puts it plainly. A boat's entry price is lower, but the cash burn does not stop. You are not building equity, just paying for the privilege to stay afloat. The numbers suggest that while a boat may cost less upfront, the monthly commitment is relentless. For anyone expecting a bargain, the math can be sobering. The true cost of entry is only the beginning. Staying afloat is another story entirely. A marine surveyor puts it bluntly. A boat demands attention every single week or you will pay for it later. Maintenance is not a side note. It is the center of boat ownership. Industry guides and seasoned liverboards point to the 10 to 20% rule. Expect to spend at least 10% of your boat's value each year just to keep it seaorthy. For a $75,000 sailboat, that means setting aside $7,500 or more annually, not for upgrades, but to keep systems from failing. Routine upkeep alone, bottom cleaning, zinc replacement, oil changes, rigging checks, soaks up between 60 to 120 hours of labor every year. That is more than a full week of labor, often on hands and knees in cramped, damp spaces. Salt water accelerates the clock on everything. Corrosion eats through metal fittings, sun bakes the deck, [music] and constant motion loosens hardware that would last decades in a house. Annual haul outs, bottom paint, and diver cleanings are non-negotiable. Skip a season and barnacles or blisters can turn a manageable job into a repair that can cost 5 figures. Engine maintenance is not optional. Neglect a cooling hose or impeller, and a minor leak can become a catastrophic failure overnight. Every system, including plumbing, electrical, and heating, demands regular checks because a small oversight can sink the entire vessel. Unlike a house, where deferred work might mean a leaky faucet or faded paint, on a boat, neglect can destroy value and safety in a single season. The surveyor's warning is clear. The real cost of boat life is not just money. It is the relentless yearround work required to keep your home afloat. A winter storm can turn a floating home into a prison. When winter arrives, many live aboard boats must be hauled out for storage or intensive winterization. That means draining water systems, packing away electronics, and sometimes moving ashore for months at a time. In northern climates, heating a fiberglass hull is a losing battle against condensation and cold. Even in milder regions, a sudden cold snap or heavy rain can force days spent huddled under blankets with every hatch sealed and a dehumidifier running non-stop. Mobility, once celebrated as a benefit, often becomes a challenge. Boarding a boat means crossing docks slick with ice or rain, then climbing down a steep companion way ladder, sometimes while juggling groceries or laundry. For older adults or anyone with mobility issues, [music] these daily maneuvers carry real risk. A single slip on a damp step can [music] mean weeks of recovery, far from the nearest emergency room. Unlike a house, there is no easy way to adapt a sailboat [music] for aging in place. Social life also takes a hit. Marinas fill with a shifting cast of neighbors, many staying only for a season or two. Friendships can be fleeting and the sense of community is fragile. Families with children face added hurdles. Few marinas welcome kids and space for play is limited to the length of the dock. Over time, the combination of weather dependency, physical strain, and social drift wears down even the most enthusiastic liverboards. For many, these pressures unseen in glossy photos become the real cost of life afloat. Boats and houses take opposite paths when it comes to value. A typical liverboard sailboat can lose 15 to 30% of its value within the first 5 years. Even with careful upkeep, most boats drop steadily and often are worth half their original price after a decade. By contrast, the average American home has seen values rise by about 3 to 5% per year over the last decade, building equity for its owners. When it is time to move on, selling a house usually takes two to three months in a healthy market. Boats are a different story. Listings for used live aboard sailboats can linger for 12 to 18 months with sellers forced to cut prices by 20% or more if surveys [music] turn up issues. One financial planner summarizes the difference this way. A house is an investment. A boat is a lifestyle expense. Liquidity [music] is not guaranteed. Mark and Elena spent 7 years living aboard a 38- ft sailboat, [music] investing $120,000 between purchase, upgrades, and repairs. When they sold the boat, it lingered on the market for 9 months before fetching $65,000, nearly half their total outlay. James, a solo sailor in the UK, saw a similar pattern. He spent 5 years and £36,000 but only recovered £21,000 when the boat sold after one year on the market. Both describe boat life as rewarding but physically and financially demanding. This is especially true as health or work needs change. Before choosing life on the water, ask yourself these questions. Can you handle ongoing repairs and tight quarters? Are you prepared for depreciation and slow resale? Will the lifestyle fit your long-term health and financial needs? Honest self- assessment matters more than any dream. Right now, over 50% of firsttime liverboards return to land within 5 years, citing hidden costs and health realities. Neither lifestyle guarantees freedom or security, only trade-offs. In a world chasing escape, the real challenge is not choosing a vessel. It is building a life you can truly sustain. Share your thoughts below.

- Why Nobody Uses Boats Between the Hawaiian Islands: Kind: captions Language: en You're chilling in Hawaii on the island of Aahu. A friend calls you up. Hey, I'm here, too. Meet me in Maui. Great, but how do you get there? Your first thought is probably to take a ferry, but that's impossible. Hawaii is a pretty unique place. It's the most expensive state to live in. It has its own official language besides English, and it's the only American state made up entirely of islands. Hawaii has a total of 137 islands. Eight are the main ones, but only six are open to tourists, and the distance between those islands is probably bigger than you'd expect. For example, traveling from Oahu to the Big Island is around 185 mi, which is about the same distance as Chicago to Indianapolis. You might be surprised that you can't make that trip by boat. In fact, the only ferry system that actually takes passengers in Hawaii runs between Maui and Lai. That's it. So, your only option is to travel between islands is by plane. Honestly, it's a little surprising, especially considering how many people visit Hawaii every year. Tourism pretty much drives their economy. And in the first 8 months of 2024 alone, over 6 million people visited its beautiful beaches. Knowing that, we would expect Hawaii to set up a good ferry system to make it easier for visitors to get around. Just like in Greece, this would probably boost tourism even more, bringing more money to the state. But believe me, authorities and private companies also know that. In fact, they've already tried it. Fairies were a big way of getting around in Hawaii from the 19th century up until the 1950s. Since then, there have been a few attempts to bring back a ferry service, but unfortunately, they have all failed. One of the biggest attempts was the Hawaii Super Ferry, which aimed to provide a fast, affordable boat service between islands. It could carry almost 900 passengers and even allow them to bring their own cars along. The trial run started in 2007 and people were excited, but it turned into a big fiasco. A bunch of legal and environmental issues came up like concerns about the impact on marine life, especially whales. The courts decided the ferry didn't go through the proper environmental reviews, and that caused a lot of trouble. By 2009, after tons of protests and setbacks, they had to shut it down. If a project like this comes up again, it will probably fail one more time. And there are a few reasons why it's almost impossible to set up a regular ferry system there. Let's start by talking about Hawaii's rough waters. They are deep and wild. Take the Alenoi Channel for example. It's a 30-m stretch that separates Maui from the Big Island. Its name means great billow smashing channel and it lives up to that. It's over 6,000 ft deep, making it one of the most dangerous stretches of water on Earth. The wind there is also super strong, the fastest in Hawaii's coastal waters. But it's not any easier in the rest of the archipelago. The whole region gets hit by storms from the Pacific up north, sending big waves crashing into the islands and making the waters rough and unpredictable. Swells can come from three, four, or even more directions, making the sea feel really choppy. Someone who's not used to taking ferry boats would definitely find that, well, uncomfortable, to say the least. To deal with rough seas like this, the fairies have to be really big and strong. But that also makes them more expensive and harder to run than the ones in places like Alaska or Washington State. Another reason it's so tough to set up this system today has to do with marine life. A big ship moving through the shallower waters when docking could really damage the local ecosystem. And there's also the problem that the seaw routes are also the migration paths of many sea animals like humpback whales and spinner dolphins. Not to mention fairy services can make it easier to spread harmful plants and animals. what experts call invasive species. See, Hawaii's ecosystem is super unique because it's been isolated out in the middle of the Pacific. When we talk about plants and animals that are native to Hawaii, we mean they got there on their own. No humans involved. They arrived with the help of waves, wind, or even birds flying over. But events like these are super rare. So for over 70 million years, their plants and animals didn't have to deal with outside competition. That's made them really fragile. So when new species get brought in by us, it can be a big problem and they could end up harming the environment. Hawaii's already been battling invasive species like fire ants, ki frogs, and mikonia, a fast growing plant from South America that takes over native forests. People there worry that fairies, which carry cars and cargo between islands, could accidentally spread these invaders even more. Like we mentioned at the beginning of the video, the long distances between their lands don't really help either. For example, if you were to travel from Oahu to the big island of Hawaii by boat, it would take around 30 hours, and that's without any stops. The same trip by plane takes about 50 minutes. Fairies that cover long distances are much more expensive, so they need a much bigger demand to make them profitable. On top of that, the state doesn't really have the infrastructure like enough ports and terminals to support the operations. So setting up new routes is pretty much out of the question. Airplanes, on the other hand, are a different story. The air travel industry in Hawaii is already well established and super competitive. That means flying is still the most practical option in Hawaii. Planes are quick. They don't make you seasick and are pretty affordable. Usually costing around $100 for a round trip. Okay, but what do the locals think about all this? Well, apparently there isn't a huge demand for residents to commute between the islands all that often. Aahu is the busiest island in Hawaii with about 70% of the state's population living there. It is where Honolulu, the capital, is located and where most of the state's business happens. So, people from the either islands usually come over for a specific doctor's appointment or to catch a big concert. And that might happen once or twice a year, no more than that. So, even if people could choose between taking a ferry and a plane for these special occasions, most of them would probably still prefer flying. There are plenty of options between the main island airports. Planes usually start flying around 5:00 in the morning, and there are flights available every hour until the last one, which is usually at 9:00 in the evening. If you've been dreaming of going to this paradasical place, but are worried about spending too much on plane tickets, I have some tips for you. First off, keep in mind that visiting all the main islands in 5 days or less is way too ambitious. So, if you don't have much time to spend there, think about your priorities. Each of the six islands open to tourists has its own vibe and activities. But you don't need to visit them all in one trip, and you can save some money by sticking to fewer islands. You can still explore the diversity of the islands by picking two that offer a nice contrast to each other. For example, while Aahu has that bustling city energy, Kauaii has a much more relaxed vibe. Also, flights in the morning are usually cheaper, and it might be better to fly on a weekday rather than the weekend, although the price difference isn't usually huge. Hopefully, these tips will save you some cash while still having an unforgettable island hopping adventure in Hawaii. Don't forget to share this video with a friend who's also dreaming of exploring these amazing islands. That's it for today. So hey, if you pacified your curiosity, then give the video a like and share it with your friends.

- Boat vs House Ownership — The Costs No One Calculates: Kind: captions Language: en A $400,000 house and a $400,000 boat are not comparable assets. One stabilizes over time. The other accelerates cost. Ownership is not price. It's life cycle cost behavior. And once you map the real numbers, the maintenance reserves, the depreciation curves, and the exit friction, the gap between these two decisions is far wider than most people ever calculate. For a clear financial comparison, every recurring cost must be mapped as a percentage of asset value, not just as raw dollars. Residential ownership begins with property tax, which falls between 1 and 3% of assessed value each year. Most USA homeowners pay around 2%, but high tax states can push this higher. Homeowners insurance averages $2,267 annually for a median home, though premiums can range from $1,500 to $4,500 depending on location and risk exposure. Maintenance and repairs are typically budgeted at 1 to2% of market value per year with rare spikes to 3% for major work like roof replacement. These costs combined to create a predictable annual outlay with most households allocating a fixed percentage of home value to cover taxes, insurance, and ongoing upkeep. Boat ownership, by contrast, operates on a different scale. Marine insurance is priced at 0.5 to 1.2% of hull value annually, rising with coastal exposure or vessel age. But the real cost driver is maintenance. Industry standards recommend reserving 5 to 7% of the boat's value per year for routine and preventive work. This figure includes hull, rigging, engine, and systems upkeep, and it reflects the continuous nature of marine wear. Unlike a house where maintenance is periodic, a boat systems require ongoing intervention. Dockage and marina fees are not a percentage of value, but they function as a fixed monthly burn rate that can rival or exceed a mortgage payment. When planning, the difference is stark. A house absorbs 1 to 2% of its value in maintenance, while a boat consumes up to seven times that rate year after year. This is the cost system divide. Home ownership and boat ownership move in opposite directions when it comes to long-term value. United States residential property has delivered a consistent upward slope with single family homes appreciating at an average of 3 to 5% per year over the past three decades. In practical terms, this means a house purchased for $400,000 is expected to gain 12,000 to $20,000 in value each year, compounding over time. Over 10 years, that same house could be worth 30 to 50% more even after accounting for market cycles and inflation. This is the foundation of equity build. The asset not only holds its value, but typically grows it, providing a hedge against both inflation and future housing cost increases. A new cruising sailboat or catamaran follows a sharply different curve. The moment it leaves the yard, the average vessel loses between 20 and 25% of its value in the first year. A $400,000 boat is commonly worth only $300,000 to $320,000 after its first year. By year 5, cumulative depreciation reaches 35 to 45%, cutting a $400,000 purchase down to $220,000 to $260,000. Even with meticulous maintenance, after the initial drop, depreciation continues at a rate of 3 to 5% per year. Unlike a house, there is no historical pattern of boats regaining value or tracking inflation. The result is a steady erosion of capital with no realistic prospect of reversal. A house stores value. A boat consumes it. This divergence is not a matter of taste or timing. It is a structural reality that drives every downstream cost and risk decision in the ownership cycle. Every major system on a boat operates on a compressed replacement schedule with costs that arrive in large unpredictable waves. An inboard diesel engine, the heart of most cruising sailboats, typically requires a full replacement or rebuild after 8 to 12 years. The outlay runs between $15,000 and $30,000 depending on size and installation complexity. Standing rigging, the cables and fittings that hold the mast in place, must be replaced every 10 to 15 years at a cost of $8,000 to $15,000. A hole refit, which includes repairs to gel coat, structural reinforcement, and sometimes full repainting, is inevitable after 12 to 20 years. This event can cost 20,000 to $40,000 for a midsize vessel. These cycles are not theoretical. Insurance companies often require proof of recent rigging or engine work before binding a policy on older boats. By contrast, major home systems fail less often and are spaced further apart. A typical asphalt shingle roof lasts 20 to 25 years before replacement with costs ranging from 10,000 to $20,000 for a mid-range single family home. Central HVAC systems run 12 to 15 years with replacements falling between $7,000 and $12,000. These events are significant, but they are rare and can often be anticipated years in advance. The difference is not just in the price tags, but in the frequency and predictability. Boat systems fail continuously, not episodically. The owner must reserve a far higher percentage of asset value each year to cover these shocks. Where a homeowner can smooth expenses over decades, a boat owner faces a near constant threat of large unplanned capital outflows. This is the underlying reason marine industry standards call for a 5 to 7% reserve compared to 1 to 2% for homes. The cash flow reality is simple. A house store's value. A boat consumes it. Monthly cash flow defines the true depth of the commitment. At the $200,000 tier, a typical US homeowner faces a median monthly outlay of $1,650 per month. This figure bundles mortgage, property tax, insurance, homeowners association, and maintenance spread over a predictable annual cycle. step up to $400,000 and the median monthly cost is $3,300 per month. At $800,000, the number reaches $6,600 per month. These bands reflect national averages, but the structure remains stable and most costs are either fixed or escalate in a controlled linear way. Boat ownership, even at the same purchase price, operates on a different axis. For a $200,000 vessel, the median monthly burn rate is $2,300 per month, already several hundred above a comparable house. At $400,000, the boat's monthly cost rises to $4,200 the boat requires $8,500 per month. These numbers include dockage, insurance, fuel, provisioning, winterization, and a maintenance reserve that absorbs the relentless pace of marine wear. Unlike a mortgage, dockage is not a path to ownership. It is a permanent non-reoverable expense. Dockage variance further exposes the volatility in boat cash flow. In the Northeast, median slip fees reach 1 800 per month, while the Southeast averages $1,200 per month. West Coast marinas command $2,200 per month for the same birth. These differences are not marginal. They can swing the total monthly cash requirement by 30 to 50% depending on region. For a boat owner, the combination of high baseline costs and regional dockage volatility creates a persistent cash hole. At every tier, the boat's recurring burn rate exceeds that of a house. The cash flow system is not only larger, but structurally less predictable. This is not a matter of preference or discipline. It is the direct result of how each asset consumes capital over time. Selling a house in the United States is a liquid transaction by global standards. Median days on market for single family homes sits at 35 days nationwide. Broker commissions range from 5 to 6% of the sale price and the buyer pool is deep over 130 million United States households. Mortgage financing is widely available with 80 to 90% of buyers qualifying for loans. The process is standardized with predictable timelines and low risk of failed closings. Boat owners face a different reality. The median days on market for a used cruising sailboat or catamaran ranges from 90 to 180 days, 3 to five times longer than a house. Broker commissions are higher, typically 8 to 12%, eroding more capital at exit. The buyer pool is far smaller. About 12 million active boat owners nationwide and only 40 to 55% of buyers can secure marine financing. This combination of long listing periods, high transaction costs, and limited access to credit creates a liquidity drag that amplifies the total cost of ownership. When exit friction is factored in, the boat's cost system becomes even less forgiving. As vessels age, upkeep costs do not plateau. They accelerate. Industry data shows that annual maintenance on older boats rises by 25 to 80% compared to new hulls, driven by the compounding failure of systems and the need for professional labor as owner mobility declines. The liveabboard market remains narrow. Only about 250,000 US households actively seek a boat as a primary residence. that limits resale options and increases exit friction. Physical access becomes a non-trivial barrier. Tasks like engine work, rigging checks, and home maintenance grow more demanding with age, often shifting costs from sweat equity to paid services. Two axioms define the system. First, boat costs escalate as both the vessel and owner age. Second, the longer the hold, the higher the capital erosion. These are not lifestyle risks. They are structural facts of the cost system. Today, the numbers remain clear. A house stores value. A boat consumes it. Boat systems fail continuously, not episodically, demanding$10,000 to $30,000 in annual maintenance and causing rapid capital erosion. Exit liquidity is thin. This decision is cash flow math, not aspiration. Choose your cost system because it will choose you back.

your yacht is listed online the charter broker is going to find you I'm a charter broker and I would describe my job as a travel agent specifically for Yachts I can book your yacht but I can book a yacht anywhere in the world I'm not tied to any one particular company and my job is to represent the client's interest in the booking process the manager is another side of it a charter manager lists Yachts on a central database and it's visible to Brokers all over the world they manage the booking calendar they hold funds in escoro account per contract terms they're going to proofread the contracts before they ask you to sign them and they're going to answer any questions from Brokers the reputation of the charter manager says a lot about the Yachts that they offer if a manager I work with all the time and do tons of volume with adds a new yacht to their Fleet I wouldn't hesitate to offer it to my clients on the flip side and a potential Pitfall is if you sign up with a yacht manager that doesn't have a good reputation or doesn't manage many other boats um you might have a beautiful new yacht excellent program but Brokers are not going to give your your yacht a chance because they don't like the person that they have to deal with when they make bookings these these names like I could tell you people in the Virgin Islands that I love and people I wouldn't work with and part of your market research would be to Charter a yacht or talk to a broker and get all of this advice throughout the process so you don't start it start this program with a disadvantage so that's a charter manager sometimes you'll find a company that is both a charter manager and a yacht manager but sometimes they're two different companies so let's talk about what a yacht manager is and what they do so a yacht manager will hire the crew they will ensure maintenance is scheduled and completed and they will make sure that the yacht paperwork is all up to date they'll rrange crew work visas and various other country registration requirements they will be your point of contact for all things related to The Yacht their job is to leaz with the owner they'll review bills for maintenance and make sure Charter funds are coming in and they should be keeping the books for you an easy way to think of the charter yacht manager excuse me the yacht manager is that they're the boots on the ground getting getting things done for you it is possible that you can self-manage or that a crew can manage the yacht but you need to ask yourself do you want to be involved in the day-to-day operations or not for the most part again everything will flow smoothly and there won't be a lot to handle from day to day but I have been a broker through hurricanes and through the pandemic which led to many many unhappy people who want to speak to who is in charge to get exceptions to their contract terms I mean these are legitimate concerns who could have seen a pandemic coming out do you want to feel those calls do you want to be the ultimate person they're talking to you at the end at the end of the phone that's negotiating with some of these Brokers or do you want the yacht manager to be able to answer this stuff for you and just give you the highlights this stuff um cannot fall to the captain to do even when it's a self-managed yacht um a captain can't be expected to have tough conversations with a potential client because if the conversation is no we're not giving you a refund you need to go on your Charter as planned you don't want there to be any sort of a you know negative connotation before this client steps on the boat so the captain can't have that conversation if they've been going back and forth and negotiating with a client that's why you would need a yacht manager that can answer some of these questions so if you're just getting started I would encourage you to find a company that offers yacht Management in addition to Charter management so again yacht management manages hiring the crew the maintenance Charter management is the calendar and the money for the charters potential pitfalls or challenges that might come up in scenarios might come up when you're in a yacht charter business so the crew is really one of the biggest aspects of running a good program a good crew will absolutely make or break your business knowing who good crew goes back to doing market research that we talked about at the beginning sometimes you're lucky enough to get an amazing crew that stays with you for 5 years they can manage the boat all the day-to-day operations and everything is smooth as can be other times you're going to get a lot of crew turnover and it might have nothing to do with you I mean maybe they get an offer for a bigger job a bigger boat that they can't resist you know one of the crew members gets pregnant a couple gets divorced they have a family emergency and need to leave the yacht all these things are completely out of your control but they affect your program at the end of the day and then of course you know how are you paying the crew are they below Market rates where you get incentives um to get incentives for good reviews or repeat clients they get an end of the season bonus I would think about what you can do to retain top talent and if they're underpaid they won't last the other thing associated with that you know crew are great we want to keep them but we also want to know is are they doing a good job one of the biggest challenges I see is is managing the relationship between the captain and the owner of course you want the crew to be great and they should be on their best behavior and best service around the owner of course you're you know you're the one employing them so past their Prime and then the owner only sees the good side and they're not listening to the Charter manager or the yacht manager telling them there's a problem this situation happens all the time I - INSANE One Year TIMELAPSE 🤯 Building Our Own Aluminum Catamaran: Kind: captions Language: en - We're building an aluminum expeditionary catamaran from scratch, our future home, and the biggest project we have ever taken on, and this is what 365 days looks like. - In April of 2024, about nine tons of CNC cut, 5083 marine grade aluminum was delivered to our shop, and we began the process of sorting and preparing the thousands of individual pieces. Of this initial nine tons. About a ton and a half would be recycled, and the remaining seven and a half tons would be become our new floating home. Each component was etched with a number, noting its purpose and place in the boat. The pieces were laid out to form the frames which span the width of the holes. We have 16 frames at all, evenly spaced one meter apart the entire length of the boat. The welding process we're using is double pulse mig welding that utilizes argon gas. Each frame was equally spaced and aligned on a strong back, which supports and holds the frames in place until they can be welded. The alignment was carefully done to millimeter accuracy using lasers. Next, each individually cut longitudinal stiffener was cleaned and placed. These stiffeners add a huge amount of strength tying the frames together and eventually giving us a surface to weld our whole plates onto the first hole plates to go on with the bottom of the bridge deck. This phase of the fabrication is done with the boat upside down, making placement and welding of the whole plates much easier using gravity. The rudder scags were single pieces and a bit too heavy for us to position by hand. This will serve as a strong point for our rudders and propeller tubes to attach to. We then began the process of placing each individual hole plate. We opted to go with six millimeter thick aluminum hole plates, giving us extra strength and rigidity. Once each plate was placed in a line, they were first T and then welded in place inside and out. For this, we use both TIG and MIG welding depending on the location. This machine is our Sial English wheel and is used to form each hole plate to a specific shape and curve based on templates provided with our kit. It took about three weeks in total to wheel all the plates. As they were finished, we welded them into place to form the structure of the boat. After about four months of solid work, our whole structure was nearly complete. We welded on the underwing girders each requiring more than 200 feet of welding on the road. These add strength and rigidity to our bridge deck and will give us a solid place to attach lifting points so we can flip her over and continue on. With the next phase of the build as the final hole place were prepared, we began fabricating the structure that would eventually become our LA on and cross beam, which holds together the bows of our cata ran. Each day we get two breaks, affectionately known as smoko. Here in Australia, we get to have a cup of coffee, a bite to eat and share a few laughs, but before we know it, it's back to the grind. We reached a major milestone when the last large hole plates went on, and we got to see the shape of our slender wave piercing bows because it's too narrow at the very front to weld from the inside. A process known as plug welding was used with all the hole plates in place. It was time to fully weld the stiffeners to the hole plates. We did all the top welds at this stage while the boat was still upside down, we also started to sand the aluminum to get the look we wanted using gravity to our advantage before we flipped her over with our date for flipping the whole fast approaching. It was time to do the final outside welding on the plate seams and a bit more sanding on those plates. That would be much harder to reach when she's right side up. Finally, we reached a major milestone in the project and the cranes moved in to flip her right side up. She was then moved back into the shed right side up so the decks and superstructure could be welded on. Before the decks were laid on, we placed the large bus bar that will become the backbone of our electrical system. The busbar is the equivalent of 500 millimeter squared of solid copper and will carry about 50 kilowatts of power from our battery bank to the electric motors in each hole. As the weeks flew by the decks and salon top began to take shape piece by piece. Delos 2.0 was finally starting to look like a boat. The roof of Delos will support five kilowatts of solar panels and the entire surface will catch water to fill our tanks. The bows are thin and meant to pierce through the waves. The first one meter or about three feet, is a very strong collision bulkhead that's fully sealed Behind that is an additional four meter watertight compartment that will eventually become my shop and storage. Soon enough, the welding of the whole plates was finished and we performed a number of tests to check the integrity. Filling the holes with water and looking for leaks allowed us to test the internal water type bulkheads and plate welds. At the very same time as the whole fabrication phase. Near completion, it was time to move her to the shed next door, which would be her home. While the interior and systems fit out phase began, our propulsion system will be a diesel electric hybrid system utilizing a nanny N four 80 diesel engine and 2 25 kilowatt electric motors from combi systems. The interior fit out officially began with the installation of internal non-structural bulkheads and supports for the flooring. KA and I are onsite every day all day. Not only do we do a lot of the work ourselves, but we constantly work with Dan and his team to refine the design and discuss ideas about the interior fit out. Internal components like floors and non-structural bulkheads are fabricated out of a plastic honeycomb material covered in a thin layer of fiberglass. It's incredibly strong impervious to water, and about one half the weight of traditional marine plywood ventilation is incredibly important for the engine rooms. We allow the engine to draw in cooler fresh air naturally to the bottom of the engine room and the hot air at the top that is not used during the combustion process is drawn out with an extraction fan. Our four peaks and doughs 2.0 are about four meters nearly 13 feet long. They're accessible from watertight doors in the cabins or from the deck. Here Dan is fabricating some very beautiful ladders for deck access through the hatches. The port hole will be are guest hole with a kids' cabin. Our galley is also on the port side, so this framework will support our induction stove, oven and galley cabinetry. Here you're seeing Sierra's kids' cabin come to life complete with bunk beds for sleepovers and lots and lots of storage. The perfect time to route crucial hoses for systems is now. So here I am routing our fuel fill, vent and supply moves, and also the hoses that will transport our toilet waste water, drinking water, and great water. All the wall liners are attached using a fast balance system, meaning they're all removable for service and maintenance of the systems and all. We really hope you enjoyed this time lapse of our build so far. It's incredible to think just how far we've come in only a year. It's a real testament to the skills of stradbroke yachts whom we feel incredibly lucky to have as our builder. We've just reached the halfway point in the project, so if you haven't ready, please click that subscribe button, or even better yet, head on over to patreon.com/sv delos where we can get more patrons only videos, early release content, and chat directly with Cas and myself about the build and all things sailing related. Thanks for watching.

I did it and here are five things you should consider before making that decision. Number one, there's nothing more expensive than an old boat. If you find a boat you're interested in, get a survey done or find someone to have a look over it who knows something about boats. Don't do what I did. Go to a local yacht club and find someone at the bar who doesn't even own a boat to give you advice. My boat is a 1983 half tunner racing boat. It's cold molded, so made of wood, and that makes it now 42 years old. Number two, you're going to have to learn to fix everything. The word marine is a bit like the word wedding. You put it in front of anything and suddenly it becomes expensive. You're going to have to learn to become a mechanic, a shipwright, a plumber, an electrician, a rigger, and if you're lucky, you might actually do a bit of sailing. It's just a reality of boats. Uh the environment is very tough and they're always needing work. Number three, you have to become a minimalist. It doesn't matter whether you got a 30ft yacht like mine or a 60ft yacht, you're not going to be able to have a huge bookshelf full of books. So, that means you just got to keep moving, which could be difficult if you're working in a location. You could try making YouTube videos, but there's 800 YouTube sailing channels already. I'm usually on the boat if the winds are over 30 knots just to make sure it doesn't drag anchor. Number five, ports rot boats and men. If you can afford to spend more on your initial your first purchase of a boat, do that. Look for boats that have had the rigging done, the sails done, the wiring done, where someone else has done all that hard work because then you can just go out and enjoy the boat and and sail. Getting an old boat where you have to do a lot of that work yourself, it's going to be very draining on your wallet and if it's all new to you, possibly on your motivation. uh you fix one thing, you find two more problems. The creation of a legend. How the aam came to be. The aam wasn't just designed to impress. It was created to redefine the very concept of luxury at sea. Launched in 2013 by Lerson Yachts, a name synonymous with super yacht innovation, the AAM has held the title of the world's largest yacht for over a decade. This extraordinary vessel was commissioned by one of the wealthiest individuals in the world with a single mission in mind to build a yacht that would surpass all others. not only in size but also in speed, elegance, and technological prowess. It took four years and over 4,000 skilled craftsmen to bring this masterpiece to life. The result, a yacht that spans an incredible 590 ft in length, yet moves with the grace of a much smaller vessel. Capable of reaching speeds up to 30 knots, thanks to a revolutionary propulsion system, the Azam is as fast as it is luxurious. Truly a testament to human ingenuity and ambition. A modern masterpiece. Let's start with the exterior. The AAM was designed with a streamlined profile which not only enhances its speed but also gives it a sleek and modern appearance. The design crafted by NTA yachts offers a perfect balance of form and function. With its long narrow hull and clean lines, the AAM cuts through the water with unparalleled grace and efficiency. From afar, it is an imposing figure on the sea, but up close, the level of detail and craftsmanship is breathtaking. The yacht's decks are expansive, offering multiple outdoor lounges and sunbathing areas. As you step inside the Azam, you're immediately greeted by an atmosphere of refinement and elegance. The walls are lined with intricate wood paneling and artwork, all chosen to complement the sophisticated color palette of creams, golds, and deep reds. At night, crystal chandeliers cast a warm glow over the space, creating an intimate and luxurious ambiance. Dining like royalty, exquisite culinary experiences. Dining aboard the AAM is an experience like no other. The yacht features multiple dining rooms, each designed to cater to different moods and occasions. Whether you're hosting a formal dinner for 50 or enjoying a quiet meal with close friends, the Azam has a space tailored to your needs. The main dining room is nothing short of spectacular with a long, elegant table surrounded by highbacked chairs, perfect for hosting lavish dinner parties. The ceiling is adorned with an intricately designed fresco, and the room is illuminated by grand chandeliers that add to the overall sense of grandeur. And of course, the yacht's team of world-class chefs is always ready to prepare gourmet meals using the finest ingredients sourced from around the world. State-of-the-art technology, innovation at its finest. But the Azam isn't just about luxury. It's also a technological marvel. Beneath its stunning exterior lies some of the most advanced marine engineering ever seen. The yacht is powered by a unique combination of gas turbines and diesel engines, allowing it to reach speeds that no other yacht of its size can achieve. This makes the AAM not only the largest, but also one of the fastest super yachts in the world. In addition to its impressive speed, the Azam is equipped with cuttingedge technology designed to enhance the comfort and safety of its passengers. conversation and start to talk to someone other times groups will be very open and there'll be lots of opportunity for you to enter the group and to enter the conversation as well how can you tell the difference how do you know which is an easy group to approach or a difficult group to approach well you need to look at the body language of the people in the group if they are standing close together if there is not much space at all in between the people in the group then that means they are very closed to anyone else entering the group it's going to be very difficult for you to enter the group and to start talking to anyone in that conversation I recommend you avoid those types of groups on the other hand if there is a group where people have gaps in between them or if they're using very open body language maybe they are not directly facing the other person they're slightly angled away from the other person this is an indication that they are open they're opening up the group for lasting impression navigating different Waters common interview formats one face-to-face interviews this is the most traditional format and often preferred in the yachting industry as it allows for a more personal connection these can take place on the yacht at a yacht club or an office setting two over the phone while less personal phone interviews are not uncommon especially for preliminary discussions or when logistical challenges arise three video calls with technological advancements and the global nature of yachting video interviews via platforms like Zoom or Skype have become more In fractional aviation, it works because it is a procedural industry. There are three pillars. First, the operator, a professional manages it. Second, [music] procedures, standards, checklists, maintenance, planning. Third, settlements. strict rules that do not depend on mood and that is why people can have shares and it can work even when they do not know each other. Yachts are different. It's about how the salon should look, how the cabin should smell, what toys, what standard. And that's why classic yacht co-ownership breaks down faster because a share often creates a need for influence. and influence breeds conflicts. In practice, it looks like this. Someone has a 20% share and suddenly wants 20% of the decisions about everything. Another has 30% and wants 30% of the decisions. And before the yacht even starts working in charter, politics begins. Interior, equipment, crew, homeport, standards, budget. This is a beautiful recipe for the yacht not to be a business, but an emotional project. And here a completely different philosophy comes into play. The yachti idea is different. You do not buy the right to arrange a yacht. You buy a minority stake in the charter business. And that changes everything because in such a model there are many more owners. So no one has the power to argue over every detail. The standard is maintained by a professional operator and you are a shareholder in the outcome. Why did it rarely work with small amounts before? Because without tokenization, co-ownership had to be limited to a few people. And a few people means companies, contracts, registers, lawyers, accounting, signatures, share transfers, then manual settlements. This does not scale to hundreds or thousands of small shareholders. Paper and administration have their limits. And blockchain does something that sounds boring, but is a revolution. It can scale ownership. Blockchain [music] provides a simple mechanism. Someone who has €1,000 euros can participate in the charter business, not as a ship owner, as a minority shareholder. In this model, the hard cap is also important. There are exactly as many tokens as corresponds to 100% of the value of the yacht. After the entire amount is sold, tokenization is closed without any additional printing. There is no risk that the profit will be divided into more shares. There is no delusion. This builds trust because everyone knows the total amount. Now we are entering the heart of the system. The smart contract has two key roles. The first role, it monitors the shares. You deposit, you receive tokens. The tokens reflect your share. This is not Excel. This is not someone forgot to add something. This is the mechanics of ownership. The second part of the same role is process security. The logic of first we collect the full amount then we buy the yacht. In other words, only collecting 100% allows us to proceed to finalization. This solves the classic problem of crowdfunding projects. What if we run out before the end? Here the principle is strict and mechanical. And now mega important. Yachtfi does not pretend to be a charter company for everything. Chartering is a specialization. Booking, marketing, customer service, standards, crew, service, logistics. The operator is supposed to deliver the operation. The earning model is simple. The charter client pays for a week for two for a cruise. This is revenue.   15. @rblom1632: 11) Make an agenda for use, and share a boat with more people. If it's in the water for 26 weeks, it makes a HUGE difference if it's in use for 2 or 18 weeks. 

I'd say: make an maxed out agenda, lend it out to friends and family. Or buy a boat with two, four or six people. 

12) install a small wood stove, now you can stretch the season by 8 weeks or go even winters sailing. Add some armaflex insulation. Beware of too cold water that can be very dangerous to fall into. 

13) More people on the boat = more sailing pleasure with same costs. 
Now you can run it 20 weeks with 4 people (=80 weeks of fun) instead of two weeks with two people. (4 weeks of fun) 

14) choose an outboard in bun. This is extra noise protection, but also weather and theft protection for your engine. Swapping a mass produced Japanese outboard is cheaper and easier than make an inboard custom fitted. If the engine dies its easy to replace with an electric outboard. That saves hours of Maintainance per year and noise. 

15) Choose a small outboard with the right propeller. The right propeller ads up to 20-30% power, with the same engine. To double the speed of a boat you generally need four times the power. With hard wind you need a propeller with traction, not many horsepower. 

16) a boat has a hull speed, so do not push the wall of the hull speed with oversized engines. In many situations a 5 horse with the right propellor will do 80% of the speed of a 15 horse with the wrong prop and wrong trim. With a 5 horse you don't need boat insurance in Holland. So double benefit. 

17) Buy Japanese engines, other countries only make CRAP. Or go electric. 

18) Choose a volume (wide/high) design with well though out interior. As many costs run per stretching meter/feet. Clever interiors save space. Choose everything compact. Foldable, stackable. Pans and cans that fit into each other etc.. 

19) Buy right sized, do not buy more boat than needed. Costs exponentially grow by length, a 30 feet is twice as expensive as a 20 feet. Go small and take a tent with you for more space. 

20) with a trailer you can choose hard anti fouling = less costs. Keep it out of the water when not in use. 
With a trailer you do not need a crane and saves also about 2x 50 eurodollar per year + 100 per year in antifouling. + 200 per year by way cheaper winter storage. 

21) Do not use folding bikes, but an electric step. Takes up less space. 
Go for an inflatable dinghy, saves spaces in winter. 

I'd personally choose a larger trailer sailer, with electric outboard, a wood stove. For 32 weeks in a Harbour with good facilities. (clean showers, close to a city center and sailing waters) with two or 4 owners + "boat friends". In winter under a roof. 

One day of polishing and work in april and one in oktober.
  16. @jimmybrokos4610: 👍🏼👍🏼👍🏼
  17. @bencoss7003: What is a coffer
  18. @markuslebt: join the facebook group "boats from 1000 dollars". it is free and you will learn something, and see some affordable boats if you're really serious about getting into sailing.

- NautiStyles Yacht Lawsuit EXPOSED – Federal Court, Missing Videos & a Dream Gone Wrong: Kind: captions Language: en It was a really emotional project for us and it meant a lot to us and that's the reason we wanted to share it with you guys, but we really were not sure how it was going to be received. >> What happens when a dream yacht project does not simply run into delays, but ends up unfolding inside a federal courtroom? >> We have safely anchored in an absolutely gorgeous location and we're ready to give you a tour of this incredible bearing. How does a bill that was once celebrated publicly turn into a dispute involving contracts, allegations of under capitalization, and suddenly disappearing videos? And how does reputational pressure change the balance of power when the people involved have a baruge and loyal? Today, we are looking closely at the ongoing legal dispute involving Victoria Challaya and Rico Rockall, better known to many viewers as the creators behind the YouTube channel Naughty Styles. We will update you on the public court filings, procedural updates, and statements that have been made on record with all allegations clearly identified as such. So, who exactly are Victoria and Rico, and why did their yacht project attract so much attention in the first place? Victoria Chilea and Rico Rock Stole are not casual lifestyle influencers who happen to discover yachts for content. Both are licensed United States Coast Guard captains with more than a decade of professional maritime experience. Their background includes charter operations, yacht consulting, brokerage work, and hands-on technical knowledge of systems and vessel operations. When they launched the Naughty Styles YouTube channel, it stood out precisely because it avoided hype. Their walkthroughs were detailed, practical, and often blunt. Viewers trusted Victoria's direct assessments in Rico's technical explanations, and that trust helped the channel grow rapidly into one of the most recognizable names in the online yaching space. With that credibility established, the next step raised an obvious question. What would happen if they turned the camera on their own dream? That question was answered when they launched a second channel now guys and announced that they were building their own long range steel explorer yacht. This was not presented as a speculative idea or future ambition. It was framed as a committed real-time project. They entered into an agreement with Bearing Yachts initially planning a Bearing 75 which later evolved into a more complex and larger vessel. Over time, the project became known as an approximately 82 ft full displacement explorer yacht named Adamus. Viewers were invited into the process at every stage. They watched shipyard walkthroughs in Turkey, listened to debates over layout and systems, and heard repeated references to the project as the biggest financial decision of their lives. This level of transparency created emotional investment from the audience, which makes the next question unavoidable. When did things start to change? The shift was subtle at first. Updates about the yacht became less frequent. Language on camera grew more careful. Details that once flowed freely were suddenly limited. While the channel remained active, the focus slowly moved away from Adamus. For many viewers, the silence felt deliberate but unexplained. The reason for that silence became clearer in mid 2025 when the dispute moved from the shipyard into the legal system. On July 30th, 2025, Anggeles LLC, a company linked to Victoria and Rico, filed a lawsuit in the United States District Court for the Eastern District of North Carolina. The defendants include Bearing Yachts LLC, Bearing Naval, and a number of related companies and individuals. According to the complaint, Anggeles LLC alleges breach of contract, fraud, unfair trade practices, and negligence connected to the construction of the vessel. It is important to be precise here. These are allegations contained in a complaint, not findings of fact, and no court has ruled on liability at this stage. That raises the next critical question. What exactly does Angelus LLC claim went wrong? Recent filings expand significantly on the plaintiff's position. According to the amended allegations, Anggeles LLC claims that the bearing defendants were under capitalized not because of market conditions alone, but because of improper transfers of assets and the siphoning of funds between corporate entities and individual defendants. The complaint alleges that this under capitalization directly prevented the defendants from meeting their contractual obligations, including timely completion and delivery of the yacht and that this failure caused monetary harm to Angelus LLC. These claims remain contested and unproven, but they add a serious dimension to the dispute. If the defendants dispute those claims, how does the plaintiff argue that the contract itself was clear and enforceable? The complaint goes into unusual detail to establish that the agreement was definite, specific, and measurable. According to Angelus LLC, the contract provided for the construction of a one-of-a-kind semicustom 77 ft steel and aluminum longrange explorer motor yacht. The agreement allegedly incorporated highly specialized requirements tailored to Angelus' operational plans, including the intention to use the vessel for charter operations. The contract also specified that the yacht would measure less than 24 m to comply with regulatory requirements. Beyond size and layout, the filings list a detailed array of specialized systems that were allegedly required under the agreement. These include high-capacity silent mode technology, solar integration, black and gray water treatment systems, and integrated high-end sound system throughout the vessel, additional high-powered alternators to support extended battery banks, upgraded Funo navigation equipment, a commercial-grade alarm and monitoring system, a professionally outfitted galley suitable for charter chefs, a hydraulic swim platform, an upgraded 2,500lb crane, a custom-designed HVAC system with integrated ceiling heaters, precision vision balance custom V EM super yacht propellers and a cathotic protection system. The plaintiff's argument is that these details make the agreement clear enough that performance obligations can be easily identified and evaluated. That leads to another question that has drawn significant attention. How much has been paid and how complete is the yacht? According to Angelus LLC, approximately 83% of the total purchase price of the vessel has already been paid. At the same time, the complaint alleges that construction of the yacht is at best approximately 70% complete. The filings further state that Anggeles LLC has fully performed its obligations under the agreement to date and remains ready, willing, and able to perform any remaining obligations if construction resumes and contractual benchmarks are met to the agreed quality standard and within a reasonable time frame. Once again, this is the plaintiff's position, not a judicial determination. So, where does the case stand procedurally as of now? As of court filings through December 8th, 2025, Anggeles LLC attempted to serve several Turkeybased defendants through the Hague Convention, which governs international service of legal documents. The Turkish Ministry of Justice later confirmed that service was not successfully completed. Because of this, Angelus LLC requested a 90-day extension to complete service, which is a common procedural step in international litigation and does not reflect a ruling on the merits of the case. At the same time, Anggeles LLC sought entries of default against several US-based defendants, including Bearing Yacht LLC and Bearing Yacht Sales LLC, alleging that they failed to respond within the required time frame after being properly served. An entry of default is not a judgment, but it does shift procedural leverage. Defense Council responded by arguing that the delays were tied to ongoing settlement negotiations and that response deadlines were intentionally delayed by mutual agreement in order to avoid disrupting those talks. No court has yet ruled on whether those defaults will stand. While all of this was unfolding in court, another development raised new questions. Why did a public update video suddenly appear and then justice suddenly disappear? Naughty guys released a YouTube video providing an update on the status of the yacht. According to viewers who watched it before it was removed or made private, Victoria and Rico stated that they had exercised their contractual right to terminate the agreement with Bearing Yachts. They asserted that the vessel belongs to them under the contract and that they are legally permitted to take possession of it and move it to a nearby shipyard for completion. They also stated that attorneys had been engaged to protect and enforce their rights and that Bearing had not yet responded to their formal notices. Shortly after the video went live, it was taken down and no public explanation has been offered. This naturally leads to another question. Was that video simply an update or was it leverage? In disputes of this scale, reputation matters. Naughty Styles is not a small or obscure channel. Victoria and Rico have a significant reach, a loyal audience, and credibility built over years. Public allegations, detailed filings, and a short-lived update video all increase pressure, whether intentionally or not. It is difficult to ignore how unusual it is for a situation involving creators with that level of visibility to escalate this far without resolution. So why has there been no full expose from their side? The most likely answer remains legal restraint, active litigation, potential non-disclosure agreements, and advice from council often limit what parties can safely say in public. In this context, silence does not necessarily mean inaction. It may simply mean that the fight has moved entirely into legal channels. That brings us to where things stand now. The lawsuit is active. Allegations have expanded. Service on foreign defendants is ongoing. Defaults are contested. Contract termination has been publicly claimed. Possession and completion of the yacht remain disputed. No court has ruled on the substance of the claims. And the most consequential questions are still unanswered, which leaves one final question worth asking. Is this story ultimately a cautionary tale about the risks of custom yacht construction? Or is it a painful chapter in a longer journey that has not yet reached its conclusion? For now, the answer sits with judges, mediators, and legal filings rather than YouTube videos. We will continue to follow the case as verifiable updates emerge, and we will bring them to you when facts, not speculation, are ready to be discussed. Thank you for watching, and we will see you in the next update.

Delays, technical disputes and build changes are very common and so are disputes, cancellations and failure to complete.  The usual guarantee of success is to use a reputable well financed builder with the usual caveats for good contract language, taking care to establish jurisdiction in case of dispute and sufficient bank and other guarantees to support any advance payment or deposit.  Unfortunately, Covid upended many contracts and businesses and whilst this was not directly foreseen many contracts allow for protection of one or both parties in such circumstances but these matters need to be considered at the outset and before any final commitments.  If the plaintiffs have proceeded to build without sufficient attention to these fundamental principles one can of course feel sorry for them, but honestly speaking as industry professionals they really only have themselves to blame, resorting to lawyers and public airing of their dispute shows fundamental weaknesses of their position as they have already created reputational damage for the builder, and therefore lost a huge amount of leverage and whilst it’s great to have lawyers this is usually not the best way to find an equitable resolution to the completion and delivery of the yacht (if that is what they still wish).

- Drugs, Sex and Death on Billionaires’ Mega Yachts | Informer: Kind: captions Language: en So I’m a deckhand on a super yacht. I basically get paid to travel around the world working for billionaires. From the Instagram and Snapchat stories, everyone seems to think that you've got the coolest job in the world. The reality of it is that you are clearing up rooms that have just had sex parties, orgies, prostitutes. Coke residue on the tables the next day. Veal or livestock so fresh that it actually gets flown in on a helicopter, slaughtered on board, and served to the guests as fresh as can be. Unfortunately, it can sometimes get a bit seedy, a bit creepy with drunk people who think they can do whatever they want. These owners, these millionaires, these billionaires, they’re incredibly powerful people, and a lot of the time, they get their own way. Drug smuggling can go on in the industry. If people wanted to be moving drugs and illicit substances, I reckon it’d be pretty easy to do. It’s an incredibly secretive world which no one really knows much about. The guests just use super yachts as their little playground. They live in this little bubble where they think anything goes, anything can happen. [Informer] [Super Yacht Deckhand] These yachts are always pretty mad, particularly the larger ones. They will pretty much always contain a pool or some sort of hot tub, equipped with a gym. Some of them have cinemas. Some of them have their own spas, helidecks, and will store a helicopter on board. The cost of these yachts can exceed anywhere about half a billion pounds. I think some of the most expensive chartered yachts on the market at the moment can actually cost four million a week. You do always secretly know that the people you’re working for haven’t always done the best things in their lives. I’ve received job offers for the owner of a boat who is one of the most notoriously corrupt people on the planet. And during the job interview, the chief officer actually asked me am I morally OK with who this person is and what he’s done. And you kind of just have to ask yourself, “Am I OK with this?” The expectations of the guests are pretty much whatever they ask for, we can deliver to them. As the day tends to go on and the more alcohol that gets consumed, their expectations go up and our ability to deliver goes down. Sexual harassment definitely does go on in the industry. You do hear quite a few stories which make the hairs on the back of your neck stand up. There is a sexual element to the job, 100 percent. It’s a very superficial industry that a lot of boats will only hire on the way you look and what waist size you are or what dress size you are, et cetera. I think they can forget that you’re here for work, you’re not here to be violated in any way. The victims of these cases are paid off. They’re told to keep their mouths shut in order for their bonuses or perks. It gives the people this right to think that they can get away with anything. There have been a few serious incidents in super yachting that have gone wrong and actually led to fatalities. One of the most famous ones in the industry was actually an anchor chain coming loose. The chain span around in what’s called the mooring bay, which is the forward section of the boat called the bow. and actually took the leg off one of the officers, and unfortunately, he bled to death and died. Basically, you’ve just got to stay really switched on at all times, which can be a big part of the reason why working in the industry can make you so tired and run down. I did a pretty long stint working on a charter boat. It was nearly 130 days of season, back-to-back charters, and within that time, we only had one day off. At the end of that, when I actually left that boat, I felt like I was ready to leave the industry because it was just killing me, essentially. You can burn out really quickly. And you can look at guys who are, you know, they’ve been in yachting for ten years or so, and they look pretty weathered. Their skin’s destroyed by the sun. The lack of sleep has just f***ed them over. And it can really have quite a physical effect on your body, and it's pretty visible. The reason that we’re so busy is because obviously we only have a certain number of crew cabins on board. You’re always in a cabin for two, if not four on some bigger boats, and if not six on some super large ones. Cabin fever is definitely, definitely a real thing. You can feel cooped up like a caged animal sometimes, and personal space becomes a serious issue. Working in yachting definitely teaches you a lot of mental fortitude. You know, you work very long hours with little rest, and that teaches you how to be resilient and just kind of push on, push on through. Obviously, there are some negative effects on your mental health. I don’t really understand the mindset of a billionaire, but when a lot of money is involved, it changes people, and I think it always brings out the worst in a lot of people’s personalities.
