These homes weren't just places to sleep or escape. They were symbols, reflections of taste, ambition, [music] heartbreak, and legacy. From the shining waters of the Ionian Sea to [music] the heart of Manhattan, the Onassis family mansions tell a story of old money, [music] luxury, personal triumphs, and private sorrow. So, let's step inside the world [music] of Aristotle Onassis and Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, and explore how their mansions became monuments [music] to love, loss, and legacy. The world of Aristotle [music] Onassis. To understand the mansions, we have to understand the man who built the empire behind them, Aristotle Socrates Onassis. [music] Born in 1906 in Smyrna, modern-day Izmir, Turkey, Aristotle came from humble beginnings. His father, [music] Socrates Onassis, was a prosperous shipping trader. But when the Greco-Turkish War broke out, everything changed. The Onassis family lost nearly all they owned. As refugees, they fled to Greece, leaving behind their home, their wealth, and their old life. But Aristotle, even as a young [music] man, wasn't someone who gave up. He left for Argentina in his early 20s, working [music] odd jobs before he found his way into the world of shipping and trade. By the 1940s, he had turned himself into one of the richest and most powerful [music] men in the world. He didn't just want money, he wanted status. And for [music] Aristotle Onassis, real status came from how you lived, the properties you owned, the parties you threw, and the people you entertained. Jackie's second chapter. When Jacqueline Kennedy, the widowed first [music] lady of the United States, married Aristotle Onassis in 1968, the world gasped. To some, she was running from tragedy. To others, she was stepping into a new life of unmatched wealth and privacy. But to Jackie, it was both. The assassination of President John F. Kennedy had left her heartbroken and haunted by constant media attention. With Aristotle, she saw a way to protect her children, Caroline and John Jr., and reclaim a sense of peace. And Aristotle, ever the showman, found in Jackie the one thing even his billions couldn't buy, royalty by association. Their life together unfolded across an extraordinary backdrop of luxury, from London's Mayfair to the Greek Isles, from private yachts to quiet countryside retreats. Each place told a piece [music] of their story. 47 Grosvenor Square, London's elegant escape. Our first stop is at 47 Grosvenor Square, a stately address in London's exclusive Mayfair district, a neighborhood where wealth whispers, never [music] shouts. When Aristotle Onassis purchased the first-floor apartment, he transformed it into something extraordinary. The apartment stretched over 5,000 square feet with five bedrooms, three reception rooms, and a sprawling [music] library. Aristotle used this space not only as a residence, but also as a social hub. It became the London headquarters of the Onassis lifestyle, a meeting place for aristocrats, politicians, artists, and celebrities. When Jackie visited, 47 Grosvenor Square would come alive. The drawing rooms shimmered with crystal, the air perfumed by fresh flowers flown in from Paris. Guests included Lee Radziwill, Jackie's elegant sister, and members of the British upper class. The home's crown jewel was its 63-foot balcony, the only one in the entire Georgian block with such a commanding view. Inside, the design was tasteful [music] but never loud, a blend of Greek elegance and European refinement. After Aristotle Onassis's death in 1975, the apartment was later purchased by Edmond Safra, a famous Lebanese banker. Safra and his wife, Lily, continued the apartment's legacy of grandeur, hosting lavish balls reminiscent of the Onassis era. Today, 47 Grosvenor Square remains a symbol of London's high society. Jackie's New York sanctuary, 1045th Avenue. After the tragedy of President John F. Kennedy's assassination in 1963, Jacqueline Kennedy found herself living in a world that no longer felt safe. So, in 1964, Jackie Kennedy made one of the most important purchases of her life, a 15-room apartment on the 15th [music] floor of 1045th Avenue, overlooking Central Park in Manhattan. The building at 1045th Avenue was legendary. Designed by Rosario Candela in 1930, it was one of the finest examples of classic New York architecture. There were only 27 apartments in the entire building, each designed to feel like a mansion floating high above the city. The apartment itself was refined, [music] warm, and deeply personal. Jackie never chased extravagance. She preferred timeless beauty over opulence. Her living room, filled with soft colors and comfortable furniture, radiated calm. There were family photos, books stacked neatly on tables, [music] and delicate artworks. Much of the furniture had followed her from her White House days. When Jackie first moved in, she was still in deep mourning. The apartment offered her a place to grieve privately. [music] Inside, she surrounded herself with books, music, and art. She found solace in words and beauty. [music] In 1968, Jackie married Aristotle Onassis. During their marriage, she spent time in Greece aboard his yacht and on his private island. But no matter where she traveled, 1045th Avenue was her true home. When Aristotle died in 1975, Jackie began a new chapter, Jackie the editor. She became a book editor at Viking Press and later Doubleday. Colleagues described her as dedicated and meticulous, editing manuscripts [music] late into the night from her apartment. When Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis passed away in 1994, crowds gathered outside the building, leaving flowers and notes. In 1995, the apartment was sold to billionaire David Koch for $9.5 million. But for those who remember Jackie, the building remains more than real estate. It's a symbol of grace under pressure and quiet resilience. Scorpios Island, the billionaire's private paradise. If there was ever a place that captured the myth and mystery of Aristotle Onassis, it was Scorpios Island. Set in the glittering Ionian Sea, this lush private paradise became one of the [music] most famous symbols of wealth and romance the world has ever seen. When Aristotle Onassis first set eyes on it in 1963, Scorpios was a barren piece of land. Where others saw wilderness, Onassis saw potential. He envisioned not just a retreat, but a kingdom. That year, he bought the island for about $114,000 in today's money. He poured millions into transforming it. Ships arrived carrying hundreds of trees, tons of soil, and sand to create golden beaches. Soon, Scorpios bloomed into a tropical paradise with three villas, a helipad, a private marina, and luxurious guesthouses. At the heart of this paradise stood the Pink House, painted in soft rose tones. It was built for Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis after their marriage in 1968. For Jackie, this island was a world away from the chaos of New York. Here, she could finally breathe. But even paradise wasn't safe from the world's curiosity. In 1971, paparazzi photographed her son bathing nude on the beaches, a deeply humiliating invasion of privacy. In October [music] 1968, Scorpios made history when Jackie Kennedy married Aristotle Onassis [music] in a small ceremony on the island. Only about 40 guests were invited. The ceremony took place at the island's tiny Greek Orthodox chapel, decorated with white lilies and candles. In the years that followed, Scorpios became the most exclusive destination in the world. Winston Churchill, Maria Callas, Frank Sinatra, Grace Kelly, and countless European aristocrats all walked its shores. But tragedy struck when Aristotle's beloved son, Alexander, died in a plane crash in 1973. The loss shattered him. When he died in 1975, he was buried on the island next to his son. Later, his daughter, Christina, would also be laid to rest there. What began as a dream of paradise had become a private cemetery. After Aristotle's death, Scorpios eventually passed to his granddaughter, Athina Onassis Roussel. [music] In 2013, she sold it to Russian billionaire Dmitri Rybolovlev for $153 million. Rybolovlev announced [music] plans to transform the island into a luxury eco-resort, investing [music] over $200 million. But no matter how much Scorpios changes, the spirit of Aristotle and Jackie Onassis will always linger in the breeze. The chapel where they married still stands, overlooking the same endless sea. The Christina O, the floating mansion. If Scorpios Island was Aristotle Onassis's kingdom, then the Christina O was his crown. It wasn't just a yacht, it was a floating palace, a symbol of triumph built from the ashes of war. During World War II, the ship was known as HMCS Stormont, a Canadian anti-submarine frigate. In 1948, Onassis bought the tired gray ship for [music] only $34,000. To everyone's surprise, he didn't scrap it, he transformed it. Over the next 3 years, Onassis poured $4 million into refitting the frigate. When the ship reemerged in 1954, it was unrecognizable. Gone were the guns and steel decks. In their place stood marble staircases, hand-woven carpets, and grand salons glowing with chandeliers. Onassis renamed her Christina, after his beloved daughter. The Christina O was no ordinary yacht. >> [music] >> At 325 ft long, it could host more than 30 guests in 18 luxurious cabins. What truly made it legendary were the details. A mosaic-tiled pool that [music] could transform into a dance floor, a bar carved from the timbers of a sunken Spanish galleon, and a fireplace of lapis lazuli. In the 1950s and '60s, to be invited aboard was the ultimate honor. Winston Churchill, John F. Kennedy, Marilyn Monroe, Maria Callas, and Frank Sinatra all stepped onto her decks. Before Jackie Kennedy, Aristotle Onassis had a long relationship with opera diva Maria Callas. Their love story unfolded largely on the Christina O. But when Onassis met Jackie Kennedy, the dynamic changed. After Onassis died in 1975, the yacht fell into neglect. But in 1998, Greek shipping magnate John Paul Papanicolaou bought it and began a massive $50 million restoration that took 3 years. Today, the Christina O still sails and is available for private charters. Each time it glides into a harbor, people still stop [music] and stare. It is a living memory of an era when luxury had a soul. The final sanctuaries. By the mid-1970s, Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis longed for something simpler, peace. She found it not in palaces, but in quiet landscapes. In 1974, Jackie purchased a small estate in Peapack Gladstone, New Jersey. The property, once a barn, sat on nearly 10 acres surrounded by fields [music] and woods. Jackie had loved horses since she was a little girl. Locals would sometimes [music] catch glimpses of her trotting quietly along the bridal paths. To them, she was simply Jackie, another neighbor who loved horses. If Peapack Gladstone was Jackie's place of peace, then Red Gate Farm on Martha's Vineyard was her dream come true. Purchased in 1979, it sat on 340 [music] acres of pristine coastal land stretching for a full mile along the Atlantic Ocean. She hired architect Hugh Newell Jacobsen to build a new house. By 1981, the transformation was complete. The main house was designed in the Cape Cod style, whitewashed, gabled, and timeless. Landscape designer Rachel Bunny Mellon crafted the surrounding grounds into a natural haven. At Red Gate Farm, Jackie was finally free. She swam in the ocean, rode her bicycle, and hosted family picnics. Her children explored the beaches and meadows. Jackie once said, "Red Gate Farm was the one place where I can breathe." In 2019, Caroline Kennedy put Red Gate Farm on the market for $65 million, described as a rare and remarkable piece of American history. The legacy of grace. From the White House to the shores of Scorpios, from London salons to quiet meadows, the story of Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis and Aristotle Onassis is one of transformation. Their homes tell this story better than any biography. The London apartment, where elegance met influence. The New York penthouse, a refuge from grief. The Scorpios estate, a dream of freedom. The Christina O, a symbol of triumph. The farms, where Jackie found serenity. In the end, what remains isn't marble or chandeliers. It's the feeling that behind the headlines was a woman who sought beauty and a man who sought immortality. Aristotle Onassis built monuments to wealth. Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis built sanctuaries for the soul. And though both are gone, their homes still stand, quiet, radiant, and eternal, as witnesses to the idea that true legacy is not measured in fortune, but in the grace with which we live >> and the peace we leave behind. Thanks for watching. Don't forget to like, share, and subscribe to this channel. See you in the next one.

- New York City's Elite "Old Money" Neighbourhoods | Old Money Lovers: Kind: captions Language: en-US Ah, New York! The city that never sleeps. Its bustling streets, adorned with bright yellow cabs, 'I love New York' stickers, and a ceaseless sea of faces, offer a captivating spectacle. But beneath this vibrant exterior lies a world of opulence. Picture it: towering skyscrapers, a playground for financial giants; neighborhoods steeped in old-money prestige, and landmarks that pay homage to history's elite. And let's not forget the iconic Frank Sinatra, serenading these streets with 'New York, New York.' Before this city's bustling vibrancy, it was a sanctuary for America's powerful families, and their legacy lives on in its exclusive addresses. In this video, we explore New York City's wealthiest neighborhoods. Stay till the end for a glimpse of the illustrious 'Billionaire's Row.' So, grab your favorite drink as we dive into the lap of luxury in a city you thought you knew so well. The Upper East Side in Manhattan is one of the most affluent parts of New York. It extends from East 60th Street to 95th and spans from East River to Fifth Avenue. Known for its luxurious residential options, apartments built with neo-gothic architectural design, and objectively posh lifestyle, this part of the city is known as a playground for the wealthy and famous. The city first became a magnet to the elites when, in the late 1800s, American steel titan Andrew Carnegie built a mansion on 91st Street, thus transforming the entire neighbourhood. In no time, several other old-money families built their dream mansions on this side of town, further adding to its desirability. Manhattan's Upper East Side was home to several prestigious families in the Gilded Age, including the Astors, Vanderbilts, Rockefellers, and Roosevelts. This area boasted architectural masterpieces like the Astor Mansion, owned by Mrs. Astor. This mansion was where she hosted the annual ball of the 400 Society of Manhattan in the 1800s. Other historic homes in this neighbourhood were the Vanderbilt triple palace, the petite chateau built for William K. Vanderbilt and his wife, Alva, and several others. Unfortunately, all of these houses have been demolished under the guise of civilization and development; however, the city still holds its charm, and thus, the lush amenities and posh neighbourhood of the Upper East Side still attract influential people in society. No wonder the kings and queens of society and pop culture choose to call this place home. These include the Kennedys and Rockefellers and several celebrities who do not necessarily belong to this sect. Up next is Forest Hills Gardens, an affluent community built within the borough of Queens. This neighbourhood's suburban quality attracted several old-money families: Families prefer the suburbs, which are free from the hassle of many developed cities. This iconic neighbourhood is where the Super Bowl was held for several years and where the movie "Fame" was filmed, so you can imagine it is quite familiar among the affluent and influential members of society. Forest Hills is also known for having streets lined with trees, expansive parks, incredible architecture, and houses built in a peculiar style known as Forest Hills Bungalow. It was developed in the early 1900s in the manner of England's Garden villages, and as such, this exotic neighbourhood was home to the old-money elites of Queens; however, with the change in times, most of these families relocated elsewhere. But despite that, Forest Hills remains the most upscale neighbourhood in Queens. Only the wealthiest families in Queens can afford to live in such a private residential area and enjoy its fairytale setting and the many other amenities it offers. Next on this list is Riverdale, the most exclusive residential area in the Bronx, which makes it sought after by the wealthiest members of society. This area was developed on a 100-acre parcel located by the Hudson River in the Northwest section of the Bronx and was owned by a well-off businessman, William Ackerman. He envisioned transforming this bare piece of land with a great view of the Hudson River into a fancy yet humble community that fellow wealthy folks would inhabit. And thus, the work began. This vast expanse of land was initially divided into seven estates, and the construction continued for years. By the end of that decade, Riverdale's homely and medieval setting made it a great summer destination for several upscale families. This private community features hundreds of cottage-style residents, each accentuated with green trees and a serene environment that looks unreal. Being one of the most elevated points in the entire city, Riverdale offers panoramic views of the George Washington Bridge, the New Jersey Palisades, and, of course, the beautiful Hudson River. Although affluent people inhabit the entire neighbourhood, you would find the wealthiest families in the upscale subsections of Hudson Hill in the Riverdale Estates. Another part of this neighbourhood imprinted on by old money folks is Fieldstone. This part of town is filled with brick and Tudor-style mansions, which once served as vacation houses for New York’s finest. Up next is Malba, a pristine suburban neighbourhood located off the East River, between College Point and the foot of the Whitestone Bridge. This upper-class community was founded in the early 1900s by the five founders of the Malba Land Company: Maycock, Alling, Lewis, Bishop and Avis. The name "Malba" was derived from the first letters of their last names; however, this is far from what makes this community one of the most sought-after in Queens. Despite being one of the wealthiest neighbourhoods, it contains a wide range of accommodation options, ranging from normal-sized family houses to multi-million-dollar mansions, all presenting different architectural designs. The streets are well-decorated with trees, which add to the ambience of the whole community, as well as the perfectly groomed lawns by the sidewalks. While Malba is a permanent residence to many, most old-money folks with property in the area use them as vacation houses — a place to unwind and escape the craziness of the ever-bustling New York City. The following neighbourhood on the list is Todt Hill, one of the many hills located in Staten Island. What sets Todt Hill apart from the others is that it is the highest point on the Eastern seaboard, sitting at a sprawling 401 feet above sea level, and the luxurious houses and mansions built on the top of this hill. One can get splendid views of Staten Island, New York Harbor, and some parts of New Jersey. Besides the top-notch views, Todt Hill offers what many sophisticated families are after privacy, serenity, and seclusion. This mind-blowing community comes with a long list of perks, including its country club, and unlike most of the other neighbourhoods we've seen, Todt Hill is strictly residential. It is open to some of the public, but only a certain calibre of people is fit to live in this neighbourhood. For example, Todt Hill has no public transportation, so residents can only transport themselves through private vehicles. Also, there are few sidewalk markets or business startups where the less affluent can earn minimum wage. Furthermore, many of the buildings in this neighbourhood are Vanderbilt-sized and Tudor-style mansions, which have been passed down through generations. A good look at this neighbourhood would tell you one thing, and one thing alone: only the wealthy are cut out for life in Todt Hill. Back to the heart of the Big Apple, we have Gramercy, a fancy neighbourhood in Manhattan and the small private park it surrounds. This historic community is tucked between East 14th and 23rd Streets, Park Avenue and First Avenue. Gramercy Park is the only private park in Manhattan and one of the three in New York City. And just like the others, it is closed to the public, as only the residents of Gramercy are allowed in it. That is because these elite residents pay an annual fee, which grants them the key to the park and access to all its amenities. This has been the norm since the park's inception in 1844, meaning it was most likely visited by top-tier old-money families such as the Astors, Vanderbilts, and Clarks, who lived on Fifth Avenue, not far from Gramercy. This neighbourhood, which feels like a tightly knit community, features tree-lined streets bordered by brownstones or townhomes. Its five-star hotels and restaurants make it a popular destination for fancy brunches and dinners. Of course, living in an environment with fancy perks such as these comes with a considerable price tag. Finally, let us discuss the affluence of the famous Central Park South in Manhattan. This luxurious neighbourhood runs from the West Side Highway on the West Side to York Avenue and Sutton Place on the East Side of Manhattan. It is even more costly because its borders are flanked by two impressive entrances — Columbus Circle and the Maine Monument on the west side and Grand Army Plaza and the Pulitzer Fountain on the east. Not a lot of neighbourhoods in the country make such a hold statement. Speaking of bold statements, Central Park South is also part of Billionaires’ Row, a group of super-luxurious residential skyscrapers and surrounding areas. From the name alone, it is pretty evident that it contains some of the most expensive residences in the world. A good example is hedge fund manager Kenneth C. Griffin’s residence at 220 Central Park South, worth a sprawling $238 million. This magnificent home is just one of many expensive homes in the area, many of which are owned by the old-money families of New York. And there you have it – seven of the wealthiest old-money neighbourhoods in New York. Which of these stuck out to you? And which ones did I miss in this video? Let me know your thoughts in the comment section. Also, don't forget to hit the "like" button and subscribe to the channel for more videos. Thank you for watching, and I'll see you in the next one.

The average rent across the city has returned to nearly pre-pandemic levels. We built fewer homes in the 2010s than in any decade going back to the 1960s. I think the way poverty is usually portrayed is from the bottom. So, talk about homelessness, or you talk about people who cannot afford housing. I think when you see it from this very top of the sphere, it somehow is also very telling. We are not building enough housing for everybody who needs a place to live. Here’s what it’s like living in the smallest apartment in New York. Wherever your expectations are, lower them. So these buildings are kind of really soulless, in a sense. They are super standardized. The trends that I see in these properties are a lot of the same finishes and materials used. Then you would have the oak floor, which is always the best type of oak floor. And there’s really nothing that would make any of these buildings stand out, to be honest. It’s really obvious when you are there. One of the most striking things was how agents are trying to always go out of their way to tell me that no one else lives in this building, that I'm not going to have any neighbors. A lot of these Billionaires’ Row towers are investments for overseas and local people, so they’ll rent them out or they’ll just own them, have them sit empty for a number of years and resell them. It’s a safe investment. It holds value over time. But what that translates to is buildings sitting empty. There’s a trickle-down effect, so once the hyper-expensive properties become even more expensive, it raises the value of everything else beneath it. I get very often the comment that this project is kind of like a Robin Hood project, in the sense that I’m getting the views which other people or most people cannot see to the public. And it’s like being so high up, it’s cool. But then it didn’t feel good to be there, in a way. One of the very big problems of these buildings, they are casting sometimes hundreds and hundreds of meters of shadows, for example, over Central Park or over other people’s up until then sunny living rooms, or just over the streets. And so, somehow, these buildings that are not even lived in are taking away such natural things from the rest of the city as sunlight. There’s a lot of money in New York. And I think there will always continue to be a lot of money in New York. I do think it’s going to be more segregated. What I’ve learned from this project is really just how obscene differences are in society, I guess, and how this can translate into this very literal physical form in the city and how that is impacting everything around it.

Yacht owners can also expect to pay 10 to 20% of the yachts value each year simply to maintain it. Superyachts or yachts longer than 82 feet can sell for hundreds of millions of dollars. This means maintenance could be tens of millions per year. Here's what goes into this superyacht's estimated annual budget of nearly 1.9 million. - [James] Welcome aboard Snowbird. - [Presenter] Cost that up even before owner set sail. - [James] Dockage fees are very expensive. They range from $3.50 a foot to 7.50 a foot per day. - [Presenter] Dockage fees can be so high for superyachts in part because there's a large demand for a limited number of spaces, but if owners keep their yachts docked, they could face other large maintenance expenses. - All the works of the bottom of the vessel gets scummed up with oysters and clams and barnacles. A lot of these electronics, they get fouled up if they sit dormant for a long time. It's definitely better to use it than let it sit. - [Presenter] While docked, the yacht can connect to the Marina's power grid for basic operations, but when it's out at sea, this 368 ton vessel requires fuel, a lot of it. - For someone like myself, I call this boat yoga. That's the amount that's in the tank. - [Presenter] In total, this yacht can hold almost 12,000 gallons of fuel. That keeps your turbos and your after cooler from getting gummed up, and it also helps reduce oil consumption and reduce air pollution by further combustion inside the engine. This part probably costs $60, $70 to replace, but it has immeasurable value in prolonging the longevity of your motors. Maintaining the engines on a yacht like Snowbird typically costs around $60,000 a year, and you're looking at parts, time, and the materials involved to change the oils and keep everything up and going. - [Presenter] That's just below deck. Superyacht owners also need to invest in keeping the rest of the yacht in pristine condition, like the teak flooring. Teak is typically used on yachts because it's a weather resistant luxury material, but it's also challenging to maintain. - [Josh] When we scrub the teak, oftentimes it only lasts roughly a week or two depending on the traffic on board. It wants to go back to its natural color, and, you know, between rain and rinsing it down, you kinda wash away all of that, you know, hard work that you've done. Annually the teak flooring costs about $18,000 to maintain. - [Presenter] Adding on smaller expenses, you're already looking at just over $1 million. But by far, there's one cost for maintaining superyachts that completely surpasses the others, the crew. Adding in crew expenses brings the annual operating cost estimate to nearly 1.9 million a year, but that's only when there are no major repairs or maintenance while owners generally spend around 10% a year in maintenance costs for new superyachts. - [James] It's kind of a guideline, but as she gets three years or five years, or hits her 10 year, it's gonna be more than 10%. - [Presenter] Every five years or so, superyacht owners have to shell out for one of their largest expenses. - Paint jobs are sometimes from a hundred thousand to a million bucks, depending on your size of your boat. Paint is about $1,800 an inch, - [Presenter] Salt in the water and air corrodes the yachts paint. To repaint the entire yacht, it needs to be hauled out of the water, then it can take months to complete a fresh paint job. There are also other major potential expenses. And if a yacht fails in inspection, repairing or replacing it could run $200,000 or more. - Certain boats, you can't get the engines out. You have to cut a hole in the side and then reweld it, recertify the hole. Now you're into the multi-millions to change out an engine. - [Presenter] To recoup some of these costs, superyacht owners will rent out their vessels. The charter fee for this super yacht ranges from $125 to $145,000 per week, that covers the boat and the crew, but renters also need to pay for additional expenses like fuel, food, and customs fees. Plus, there's a customary tip of 18 to 25% for the crew members. At best, superyacht owners might break-even on maintenance costs with income earned from chartering. - I don't think it's a profitable situation. No, it doesn't take millions of dollars to fill up an 80ft boat. No, a 120ft boat doesn't have a crew of 50 who each make half a million a month. Hate to tell you but an inch of paint isn't $1800....for any boat anywhere. Bottom paint can run $1800 per gallon...not inch. That guy was a lying clown. It doesn't take a gallon of copper paint to cover an inch. 

The only way it costs $18000 per month to wash the teak deck is of the crew is using pick axes. Teak decks are washed each day with water...not champagne. They use special soap, not liquid gold. They use mops, not fur coats. $18000 per month is a bald faced lie. 

Again, boats are expensive. The bigger the more expense...just like a house. However, this is typical journalism....if you don't know... just make it up. Journalism is just story telling. This was a story...a false story

  2. @DisciplinePelajar: Monaco, Billionaire Playground 😎
  3. @high-reshallucinations: 1. Russian oligarch yachts
After the Ukraine war sanctions, many Russian-owned yachts were seized in places like Spain, Italy, France, and the US. Some owners moved their yachts to Turkey because Turkey did not join Western sanctions.

2. Superyachts are symbols of extreme wealth
The film shows huge yachts with luxury cabins, crews, security, submarines, private events, and weekly rental prices around €500,000.

3. The environmental criticism
A major theme is that superyachts burn massive amounts of diesel. The transcript says some consume around 500 liters per hour, making them controversial because of their carbon footprint.

4. Life behind the luxury
The documentary contrasts guest luxury with crew life. Guests have huge suites, cinemas, decks, and private lounges, while crew members live in small shared cabins and work under strict standards.

5. New luxury markets
Saudi Arabia, Dubai, Hong Kong, and Thailand are shown as growing yacht hubs. Saudi Arabia wants places like Jeddah to become major luxury boating destinations.

6. “Green” yachts
A big part focuses on Francis Lapp and Sunreef in Poland, building solar/electric catamarans. Their yacht Marie-Joseph uses solar panels integrated into the hull and electric motors, aiming to reduce diesel use.

7. The contradiction
The documentary’s title suggests “Russian oligarch yachts,” but much of it is actually about the wider yacht industry: luxury, sales, chartering, crew life, Saudi/Dubai markets, and eco-yacht technology.

Overall: it’s less a pure investigation into Russian oligarchs and more a documentary about how the superyacht industry is trying to survive bad publicity by moving into new markets and promoting “eco-luxury.”

world with a yacht far from it in the year 2000 they launched a 95 meter long super yacht with no less than three engines of 10 000 horsepower each making her not only one of the largest yachts in the world at the time but also one of the fastest in that size range with a top speed of over 25 knots incredible as it may sound ocean go have now taken that yacht and threw a painstaking process of that owners are using their yachts and the truth is that most owners simply don't need all of that speed all of the time they far prefer long distance cruising at more conservative speeds and in greater comfort so those engines had to go and at that point they decided to replace everything relating to the power of the yacht the result is nothing short of sensational two MTU 4000 series engines power project H to a still very respectable top speed of 18 knots and now a range of 6 000 nautical miles can be achieved at a cruising speed of 14 knots a conversation to the engines would be to The Cabins naturally each area of the yacht can then modify the temperature according to their requirements um and on the subject of the cabins I have to say that the look of both the interior and   16. @cruise35able: The Reality Behind the 10.5Meter Video Wall

This massive video wall, connecting the main lobby and the corridors of the H3, represents one of the most advanced display systems currently found in the yachting industry.

Technical Capability : Broadcasting footage from underwater cameras or submarine-mounted lenses onto this 10.5meters long 2.1meters high video wall is not only technically possible—it is a core design intent.

Content Versatility:

Submarine Streaming: For a "Giga-yacht" of the H3 class, a data link with a submarine (via acoustic modem or fiber-optic cable) allows the massive wall to be filled with real-time, deep-sea footage, just like on the Kismet.

Underwater Cinematic Experience: By transmitting wide-angle feeds from hull-mounted cameras, drones, or ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles) onto the 10-meter display, guests are treated to an overwhelming sense of immersion, as if the entire yacht were traveling through the depths.

The Reymond Langton Style: * Designer Reymond Langton treats displays as "architectural elements" rather than mere televisions. Consequently, when not in use for video, the H3’s video wall transforms into "digital marble" or kinetic artworks, blending seamlessly into the interior architecture.

It was designed by a celebrated American guy called Theodore Ferris, launched in 1902 in Staten Island, New York for an American called Charles Fahnestock, and he had the yacht as a private vessel to take him on his retirement, to cruise around the American waters and the Mediterranean. So, I've only done one circumnavigation on this boat. The current owner's done three, and on my circumnavigation, we started in the Med. We went down to south Georgia because the boss was really interested in the story of Ernest Shackleton and the Endurance, so we went to visit some of the places in south Georgia that Shackleton walked across with his men to try and rescue them from Elephant Island, and it was an experience. We've taken the vessel to Madagascar. We've been to the South Pacific. I remember once we were passing Sumatra, just to north of Indonesia, and we were about 200 miles west of the island, and we found two guys in a fishing boat, lost at sea. At the time, of course, piracy was pretty rife, so we were quite cautious, but then we realized, actually then, these guys are in trouble. So, we picked them up and we brought them on board. I've never seen somebody so happy to have a fresh water shower. we tried to work out from discussing with them by means of charades and Pictionary because they didn't speak any European languages, how they happened to be. It turns out they were at sea for 10 days adrift. - Abandoned Luxury Yachts, Empty Marinas — Is Dubai COLLAPSING Fast?: Kind: captions Language: en 2,100 births 95% full That was Dubai's reality at its peak. By late 2019, the city's marinas had become the undisputed center of global yacht culture, a floating empire designed to anchor the wealthiest individuals on Earth. And then, it collapsed. Today, more than 2,000 vessels sit idle. Occupancy has fallen to just 45%. Over 800 million dollars in asset value erased. Abandoned yachts now rot at their moorings, some leaking fuel into the water. And behind the scenes, more than 12,000 maritime jobs have quietly disappeared. To understand how something this massive empties this fast, you have to understand the system that was built to fill it. October 17th, 2003 A barren stretch of Dubai's coastline, nothing but sand and possibility. Construction crews broke ground on a project that wasn't just ambitious, it was audacious. A 3.5 km man-made canal glass towers on both sides A marina designed not just to compete with Monaco, but to rival it. By the end of the decade, more than 2,000 berths would line Dubai's waters, spread across Dubai Marina, Port Rashid, and Mina Seyahi. This wasn't incremental growth. This was a statement. Dubai wasn't trying to join the world's yachting capitals. It was trying to outshine them. Developers described it as a new anchor for global wealth, a place where super yachts from London, Moscow, and Mumbai would dock beneath a skyline of luxury apartments and five-star hotels. Every detail pointed toward one thing, scale. The marina was engineered to handle vessels up to 160 m in length, from everyday pleasure craft to the floating palaces of billionaires. Millions of cubic meters of sand were dredged to carve the canal. Imported palm trees lined marble promenades designed to replicate and surpass the world's most exclusive Mediterranean ports. This wasn't just infrastructure. It was engineered prestige. City officials were clear about the ambition. This would not be a quiet harbor for locals. It would be a magnet for international capital, a physical symbol of Dubai's arrival on the global luxury stage. The mission was repeated in early press releases and boardroom conversations alike. Build the most desirable address for yacht owners on Earth. And the numbers backed it up. More than 2,000 births planned across the city. Dubai Marina alone targeting over 1,000. The construction boom pulled in talent from across Asia and Europe. Engineers, marine architects, logistics crews, all contributing to a transformation that would redefine the skyline. By 2010, it was complete. Glass towers reflected across calm water. Promenades filled with movement. And the docks filled fast. Yachts registered in the Cayman Islands, Malta, and the British Virgin Islands lined the marina, a floating index of global wealth. Dubai was no longer an outsider. It had become a peer to Saint Tropez, to Port Hercule. And then came the golden era. By the end of 2019, Dubai's marinas were operating at near full capacity. Occupancy ranged between 88% and 90% with some weekends seeing every birth taken. On average, 12 new vessels arrived each month. A constant flow of capital. a constant signal of demand. Charter companies reported record-breaking performance. Industry estimates placed annual yacht charter revenue between 100 and 170 million dollars, far ahead of regional competitors. Dubai had secured its position at the top of the Gulf's luxury hierarchy. But the real story wasn't just the numbers. It was the people. Russian oligarchs, Saudi royals, British expatriates, Indian business magnates, European and American executives. For many, owning a berth in Dubai wasn't just a lifestyle choice. It was strategy. A place to store wealth, host private gatherings, and when necessary, wait out instability elsewhere. Dubai wasn't just selling luxury. It was selling certainty. A safe haven, carefully maintained, precisely managed. Then came 2020. The pandemic hit, and for the first time in years, the system faltered. Occupancy dropped to 65%. The walkways fell silent. Movement slowed. But the recovery came faster than expected. By late 2022, Dubai's marinas surged back. A wave of Russian-owned yachts redirected from Europe due to sanctions, a renewed demand for private, insulated luxury. By 2023, occupancy had climbed back to 88%, almost identical to pre-pandemic levels. Charter bookings rose by 25 to 30%. Restaurants, clubs, and service firms reported revenues close to their peak years. From the outside, it looked like a full recovery. But underneath, something had changed. The marinas looked full, but they didn't feel the same. More yachts sat unused for extended periods. Ownership structures became more complex. Flags changed, not for identity, but for protection. Crew sizes were reduced, activity slowed. Brokers began speaking quietly about clients exploring alternatives in the Mediterranean. Service companies noticed delayed payments and renegotiated contracts. The system was still functioning, but it was no longer stable because the entire model depended on a fragile balance, a continuous inflow of global wealth, the absence of regional conflict, and above all a perception. The perception that Dubai was safe, stable, untouchable. And when a system is built on perception, it doesn't need a collapse to break. It just needs a shock. February 28th, 2026. Just after sunrise, the first warning hit Dubai's marine sector. Within 4 hours, global insurance syndicates issued emergency advisories. All Gulf waters, including Dubai's marinas, were now classified as high risk. The impact was immediate. Insurance premium surged by 300% in a single morning. Policies that once cost 80,000 pounds per year suddenly demanded over 320,000 pounds if coverage could be secured at all. For yacht owners, the math changed instantly. And when the math changes, behavior follows. Lena Carlile, a senior analyst at HSBC Middle East, described the aftermath as a financial wipeout. More than 800 million pounds in asset value erased from Dubai's marinas in less than a week. Buyers disappeared. Deals froze. Lenders began calling in debts. The market didn't collapse because yachts were destroyed. It collapsed because trust disappeared. Dubai's marinas, once a symbol of global wealth, became a ledger of stranded capital. And the shock didn't stop at the water. Charter bookings were canceled en masse. Crew contracts were suspended or terminated. Service companies, from mechanics to marina caterers, watched their revenue vanish almost overnight. The entire ecosystem, built on movement, capital flow, and confidence, began to unravel in real time. What once looked permanent suddenly looked fragile. By midday, the first aerial footage began to spread. Empty berths. Idle hulls. A marina that no longer moved. What followed wasn't gradual. It was the visible aftermath of a single financial shock here, echoing through every layer of the system. 45%. That's where occupancy stood in March 2026. A number that would have sounded impossible just a few years earlier. Where waiting lists once stretched for months, nearly 2,000 vessels now sat idle across Dubai Marina, Port Rashid, The drone shots told the story Rows of empty slips bleaching under the sun. Walkways with no movement. No crew. No activity. In a city built on spectacle, the silence felt louder than anything. Because this wasn't just a number. It was physical. The camera moves slowly across what used to be a floating parade of global wealth. Now fenders scrape quietly against empty docks. Paint begins to peel on neglected hulls. The waterline collects oil and debris instead of champagne reflections. Glass towers still rise above the marina. But now they reflect emptiness. Not movement. Not life. Just absence. Berths designed for over 2,000 vessels. Half of them unclaimed. For marina operators, every empty slot is a direct financial hit. Mooring fees, once predictable, reliable, have disappeared. Service crews lose contracts. Maintenance firms shut down jobs. Cleaning teams sit idle. The ripple effect is immediate. Every yacht that leaves takes an entire chain of income with it. A mechanic. A supplier. A catering crew. A financing agreement. Gone. And when the footage went viral, something shifted. This wasn't just a downturn anymore. It was visible. Dubai had always sold luxury as something you could see. Now the collapse was visible, too. Berth by berth. Then came the exodus. Not slow. Not cautious. Decisive. AIS tracking data and port logs revealed what was really happening behind the scenes. 70% of Russian-owned yachts gone. We routed toward Turkey, Greece, and Cyprus. Moving in clusters, quietly, quickly. Out of the Gulf. Into safer waters. Brokers described it as a race. Bodrum. Mykonos. Limassol. Ports where insurance still held. Where risk felt distant. Where clients felt comfortable again. GCC owners followed. shifting towards Saudi Arabia's Red Sea coast. New incentives, new developments, new promises. Neom, King Abdullah Economic City, the next safe harbor. European-owned vessels, long a staple of Dubai's marina culture, returned to the French and Italian Riviera. Where demand was waiting, where the season was already building. American investors didn't hesitate. Instructions were sent within hours. Cross the Suez, head west. Caribbean routes reopened. St. Barts, Nassau, Antigua. The map of global wealth redrew itself in real time. One marina manager described it simply. Boats didn't leave one by one, they Familiar hulls gone overnight. Invoices left unpaid. Radios silent. What remained wasn't just empty space. It was a vacuum. Because the system had always depended on a specific kind of owner. Mobile, flexible, unafraid to move capital instantly. And when risk appeared, they left. What they left behind wasn't just physical emptiness. It was the collapse of demand. A gap no local market could fill. Within 4 months, over 12,000 jobs disappeared from Dubai's marine sector. Not slowly, all at once. Crew members, cleaners, technicians, caterers, entire teams erased. One marine ecologist described it as a chain reaction. When the yachts left, everything connected to them vanished, too. The impact didn't stop at employment. Infrastructure began to degrade. Out of 320 navigation buoys, nearly half were left unmaintained. Drifting, rusting, out of position. What were once precision-managed waterways became unpredictable. And beneath the surface, the damage was already spreading. Fuel leaks increased. An estimated 3,200 L per month. Water clarity dropped sharply. Turbidity levels rose by nearly 50%. Oxygen levels fell. Marine life began to suffer. Algae spread across the hulls of abandoned boats. What had been a showcase of luxury was becoming an environmental risk. And with every passing week, recovery moved further away. But the damage didn't stop at the waterline. It reached the banks. By spring 2026, financial filings began to reveal the next phase. Defaults. Luxury yacht loans. Marina front property debts. In just 3 months, over 1.1 billion pounds in non-performing marine loans surfaced. Higher than anything seen even during 2008. Liquidation notices piled up. March, April, May, one after another. Banks suddenly held assets no one wanted to buy. Repossessed yachts with no bidders. Luxury apartments with no demand. And the property market followed. Penthouse prices in Dubai Marina dropped by up to 40% from 2025 highs. Sellers outnumbered buyers 10 to 1. Even prime waterfront units sat empty. Months without offers. The illusion cracked. Because the entire system had been built on a belief that global wealth would always flow in. That demand would always exist. That Dubai's position was permanent. Now that belief was being tested. And as it weakened, the consequences spread outward. From marinas to real estate to banking. From perception to reality. And while Dubai's docks emptied, somewhere else they were filling. Mediterranean ports surged. Monaco, Antibes, Bodrum, superyacht arrivals increased by 15% in a single season. Waiting lists grew longer than ever. Athens, Palma, charter brokers overwhelmed with demand. Ports expanded capacity. Permits fast-tracked. New berths approved. Seasonal crews hired at scale. The global circuit shifted. Even major events adapted. The Cannes Yachting Festival expanded overflow showcases. The Monaco Yacht Show stretched beyond its usual limits. And Dubai, its own boat show, once a fixed point in the global calendar, quietly canceled for 2027. No announcement with impact. No dramatic headline. Just removed. And in that absence, the shift became undeniable. This wasn't just a temporary disruption. It was a redistribution of capital, of confidence, of belief. The same system that had built Dubai's marina empire was now rebuilding it somewhere else. By 2032, current trends suggest only one in five berths may see consistent use. The rest left exposed to heat, salt, and time. Maintenance costs rise. Environmental risks compound. And the image begins to shift. From luxury hub to ghost port. The ecosystem that once supported thousands of businesses, charter firms, service crews, waterfront retail, doesn't collapse in a single moment. It fades. One contract at a time. One storefront at a time. One empty dock at a time. And then there's the worst-case scenario. Already visible in pockets of the waterfront. If current abandonment continues, more than 60% of Dubai's prime marina space could stand empty within a decade. A city that defined itself through spectacle becomes a case study in how quickly spectacle can disappear. Because what's happening here isn't just about yachts. It's about trust. When trust breaks, capital moves. Vast. Faster than infrastructure can adapt. Faster than policy can respond. Faster than perception can recover. Yacht culture didn't just decorate Dubai. It anchored an entire promise. A promise of safety. Of stability. Of permanence. Now that anchor is dragging. And what's left behind isn't just empty berths. It's a warning. Written in salt, steel, and silence. So the question isn't just what happened to Dubai's marinas. The real question is what happens to any city when the image it built its future on stops being believed? Let me know your thoughts. Where are you watching from? And do you think Dubai can rebuild this kind of trust again?

- Levels of Wealth: Inside The Secret Lives of The Ultra-Rich: Kind: captions Language: en-GB If you earned $1 per second. Then to make 1 million dollars would take you about 11 and a half days. But to make 1 billion dollars, would take you over 31 and a half years. You see, it’s easy to think of wealth in terms of rich or poor, but broadly speaking, there are arguably 5 different levels of rich, and at each level, things change very drastically. The difference between what a millionaire can do, and a billionaire can do, is simply staggering. In this video we’ll be exploring what life is like at each of these different wealth levels, and delving into how the ultra-rich spend their money, including some of their most bizarre and extravagant purchases. But, as you’re watching, you’ll realise that one of the biggest changes that happens at each wealth level, is perspective. Because prices feel relative to how much you earn. So if you compare someone whose earning $40,000 a year with someone earning 400 million a year, it’s 10,000 times more. Which means: A lambo that costs $300,000, to them is the equivalent of spending $30. A $10 million piece of art? Feels like $1,000. A $50 million home in the Hamptons? Feels like $5,000. So whilst to us those purchases would feel so major, to a billionaire, it’s not even something they need to think about. And of course, when you’re that wealthy, it’s not just items and experiences you can buy. But we’ll get to that. Let’s instead start off, with the first level of rich: The Top 1% Even though this phrase is synonymous with being extremely rich, if your income is over about $200,000 a year, you would technically be part of the top 1% of worldwide earners. And so being in the top 1% may not make you feel quite as rich as you think. Because sure, you can typically afford a nice house and car, but with those things comes higher costs, and so you probably don’t feel very financially secure, and you’re likely still working a full time job. Which is why beyond this level, wealth starts being measured in net worth rather than income, as the more wealth you have, the more assets you have, and you’re probably not making your money from a salary. So the next level of rich is a Net Worth of $10 Million - $30 Million At this level, your needs are met. You can live very comfortably. You have a really nice house. You often fly first class internationally. If it’s a special occasion, you might book a $2,000 suite. You can afford any healthcare you need, and in fact no emergency financial situation can destroy your life. But you are still not "rich" in the way that money doesn't matter. You still have to be sensible and careful with big decisions. And in the banking world, you’re still not classified as ultra high net worth'. Which brings us to out next level: Net Worth of $30 Million - $100 Million At this point, you start playing in the big leagues. And most likely you run or have a controlling interest in a very large company. You always stay at 5 star hotels, and you have multiple residences all around the world you can stay at as well. You take holidays during prime time, for example going to Monaco for the Grand Prix, or Cannes for the film festival. And you won’t bat an eyelid when the place you’re staying at costs $10-$20,000 per NIGHT. You have personal assistants, and are starting to have ‘people’ that others have to talk to to get to you. You can buy pretty much anything that normal people think of as ‘stuff rich people buy. Like you can get any cars you want, the fanciest jewellery, and so on. Instead of flying first class, you fly private. Although you’d typically charter a flight or own a private jet fractionally through a service like Net Jets, instead of buying it outright and having to maintain it. But at this level, your social circle changes dramatically too. You probably socialize with Congressmen, Senators and community leaders. You are likely very well respected within some very high class circles. Interestingly though, since you’re likely surrounded by other ultra wealthy people, you may still feel you don’t have enough. Sure, you may have just bought a new Ferrari, but your friend has a limited edition version where only five of them were ever made. Sure, you just had dinner with a local governor, but your billionaire friend just had dinner with the President. Which brings us to our next level of wealth: Net Worth of $100 Million - $1 Billion To be at this level, you likely have ownership or control of a business that most of the public have heard of, even if they haven’t heard of you directly. At this point, you can likely socialize with movie stars, rock stars, and the corporate elite. You can go pretty much anywhere you want, and always in style. You have multiple residences with staff, all over the world, with elite cars at every residence. If it’s your thing, you might have a private island. And of course a private yacht to get you there. Almost any experience you can dream up, you can have. Want to play tennis with your favourite tennis star? Call his people. For a donation of 100k+ to his charity, you can probably play a match with him. Want your favourite band to come and play at your private party? For the right price, they will. Love art? Your people could arrange for the curator of the Louvre to show you around and show you some masterpieces they haven’t exhibited in years. If you can dream an experience up, you can likely have it - donations and fees can get you to anyone. Once you have many hundreds of millions of dollars, it’s the same when buying things - you may hire people to find you the most extravagant versions of items possible. For example, the Australian company Toilet Paper Man produces 22-carat gold toilet paper, for a cost of over 1.3 million. And if you are going to quite literally flush money down the toilet, why not get a gold toilet or entire gold bathroom to go with it? In fact, if you’re ultra-wealthy, you can literally have gold everything. How about a 24 karat gold water bottle, which in itself is a piece of designer art, and comes with spring water from France and Fiji, combined with glacier water from iceland. Or alternatively, how about the world’s most expensive iPhone has 24 carat gold casing, and more than 600 white and black diamonds, and sapphire glass for the screen. This certainly isn’t available to the public, and to buy one you’d need over 15 million dollars. At this level, it’s not just about buying the fanciest most luxurious items, it’s about getting the rarest and most exclusive things you possibly can. You could get a limited edition pen, that costs $160,000, has 4,810 diamonds inside, and only 8 are made each year. Or if you want to go a lot bigger than that, many billionaires turn to art. Not just rare paintings, but things like this dead shark art piece created by Damien Hurst that sold for $12 million dollars. Or let’s say you like piano? Well, here’s the same crystal piano that was used for the Beijing Olympic Games. It was purchased at auction by a private bidder for a record $3.22 million U.S. dollars! Your mind is the only real limit to what is available. But Our final level, $1 Billion Net Worth and Above. Before we get into what you can do as a billionaire - which is pretty insane - it’s worth noting that the number of billionaires on Forbes' annual rich list has significantly increased in recent years, and there’s now around 2,755 known billionaires. Of course, it’s possible there are more who have generational wealth that simply do a good job of staying very very private. Either way, Forbes estimated these 2,755 billionaires are worth a combined $13.1 trillion, and therefore have more wealth between them than the poorest 5 billion people on earth combined. In fact, Oxfam have argued that the very richest 26 billionaires have more wealth between them than the poorest 50% of the entire world. These statistics are overwhelming, but let’s actually visualise it to put things in perspective. This rice example really highlights that even within this billionaire category, the difference between 1 billion and the kind of wealth Jeff Bezos or Elon Musk has, is insanely vast. I’m a big fan of James Jani’s YouTube channel, and he also did a great job of visualising the difference between these large sums of money in a different way: So, now that we’ve got a better idea of the scale of a billion dollars, let’s look at what you can do with it. Because essentially, you can buy anything. And I do mean, anything. We’re no longer just talking about luxurious items and experiences. Firstly, you can buy access. You can ask your staff to contact almost anyone on the planet and get a call back. It’s amazing the level of access and respect a billion can get you, where within an hour you could ask your people to get in touch with someone, and they’d be able to do it. It’s not just celebrities either, it works the same with powerful people within politics too - especially because the billionaire may well be a donor to their political party. And so, if you’re a billionaire you may occasionally meet with heads-of-states and have conversations with them. Which brings us to the second thing a billion dollars can buy: Influence. As a billionaire, you have many ways to shape public policy and the public debate. This isn’t always done in some kind of evil way, like lobbying for lower taxes for rich people (although that obviously does happen). But because of your wealth and connections, you can absolutely influence big changes in the world, and not just on a political level. Because Another thing you can buy is IMPACT, through philanthropy and charity. Your money can literally change countless lives. The question is, how do you distribute it? Clean water for villages? Fund new hospitals? Try to save a dying species of animal? With money, you can impact almost anything of your choice. But one of the biggest differences with billionaires that is often overlooked, is the ability to essentially buy time. You quite literally don’t have wait for anything. No lines. No sitting around. Just think about it: You’ve got your own private jet, so you just show up at the airport whenever you want, sit down in the plane and 2 minutes later you’re in the sky and can go where you wish. When you arrive, a driver will have been arranged to pick you up and take you wherever you want to go. Your assistant will have already got you the best table at the restaurant you mentioned wanting to eat at. Or maybe your assistant arranged for a celebrity chef to come to one of your vacation homes instead, at whatever time you choose. However… believe it or not, there are some downsides to being this ultra wealthy. Firstly, it’s nearly impossible to have normal emotional relationships at this level. It can become increasingly difficult to have friends and family that love you for who you are. They exist, but it is pretty hard to know which ones they are. Because, your world is likely filled with yes men, and people looking to take advantage at every opportunity. Your trust and connections with people are undoubtedly considerably weakened. Dating also becomes an odd experience. On the one hand, some of the world’s most beautiful and intelligent women are all around you at parties and events, but again, finding someone you have a genuine emotional connection with is a different story… especially because you’re time is so valuable that you’re always busy and unable to invest in making deep connections with new people. And even if you do find someone, the relationship balanced is skewed in a strange way when you have essentially unlimited money - no matter whether you pay for everything or whether your partner makes their own money, either way something feels off. And finally, and most crucially, no level of money can give you everything. Death is the great equalizer, and losing loved ones, and losing your own health and facing your own mortality, are problems that no amount of money can solve. The reddit poster who inspired this video and deserves full credit, talks about how he met Sylvester Stallone at a party, who has obviously had a very successful career. He said he was such a great guy, had huge levels of wealth and luxury, a smart and beautiful wife. And yet, he also had a special needs son who died young. In his words, Nobody has it all. Nobody. In fact, if you were watching this video and feeling poor, I think it’s worth re-thinking. Because in this video we’ve looked at several levels of wealth, but the fact you are watching this means you are nowhere close to bottom of the total wealth pyramid. It’s easy to look up and feel frustrated that some people have so much more than us, but just look down the wealth pyramid for a moment. If you have internet access to watch this, plus some food in the fridge, clothes on your back, a roof over your head and a place to sleep… … then you are richer than 75 per cent of this world. In fact, even if you have $1 to your name right now, you are still richer than the majority of the world who are either in debt, or have absolutely nothing. What this video really highlights is that rich vs poor is so arbitrary. It’s not two groups, there are many many levels. And compared to most of the world, you might well be a lot richer than you think… If you enjoyed this video, please make sure you subscribe and turn on notifications for some future mini-documentaries that are on the way. Thank you for watching to the end, you are a legend, and I’ll see you next time. Cheers!

- The Empty Boat [Short Story on Self Awareness] - A Zen Motivational Story: Kind: captions Language: en-US The Empty Boat A Zen Story on Self-Awareness. in a picturesque village in Japan, there lived an old fisherman named Takeshi. He was known for his spiritual beliefs and profound understanding of life. He spent days sailing fishing in the serene lake near village. One foggy morning, while Takeshi was out on the lake, he noticed another boat rapidly approaching in the dense fog. He called out a warning, but the boat kept coming closer. It crashed into Takeshi’s boat. He managed to maintain balance, his boat damaged. initially upset ready yell at that boat’s owner. However, as the fog cleared, he realized that the other empty. It had come loose from its moorings drifted across the lake. realization no one blame or to direct his anger at made pause. His anger disappeared, replaced by an smile. Later day, when he was asked about the incident by his fellow villagers, used it as a lesson. said, “In life, we often encounter empty boats. We meet situations us, and we react with anger because assume there’s someone blame. sometimes, no one at end, intending cause harm, just like boat.” continued, “Our such only disturbs our own peace. If we can recognize accept some things happen aren’t anyone’s fault, our peace respond more wisely.” The villagers took heart words, story of the “empty boat” became a lesson self-awareness They not every affront is intentional, reacting with serves purpose other than disturbing one’s own This Zen provides a perspective managing promoting self-awareness. highlights the importance of not taking personally preserving of mind face adversities. Like Takeshi, we can learn to see ‘empty boats’ in our lives rather unhelpful anger.

This sovereign wealth fund is one of the largest in the world with assets reaching an astounding $335 billion. That's right, billions. But how do they spend all this? Well, they've got their fingers in nearly every pie you can imagine. The Althani family doesn't just invest in one sector or country. They are a global force. Their sovereign wealth fund has made massive investments across industries such as real estate, sports, and infrastructure, making them one of the most powerful financial players in the world today. The lifeblood of Qatar's wealth. But how did this royal family get to such heights? Look no further than the natural gas reserves. Qatar is one of the world's largest exporters of liqufied natural gas LNG and this resource has been the true engine driving the nation's prosperity. In fact, Qatar holds the world's third largest reserves of natural gas and it has become the lifeblood of its economic power. The country's natural gas reserves allow Qatar to produce LNG at an extraordinary scale, creating wealth that has transformed the nation into a global financial powerhouse. The Althani family's wealth was built through this resource, which continues to fuel the country's economy today. Natural gas provides the family with immense riches and allows them to exert control over global markets in ways few nations can. For the Alani family, the rise to power has been a strategic move, a mix of investing in the right resource at the right time. entry that capital investment is massive but that also keeps competition low unlike traditional businesses shipping doesn't rely on local markets it's a global business where the best operators make with buying new ships older ships can be bought at a fraction of the price of new on Savvy ship owners buy ships during recessions when prices are Rock Bottom take John fericon the Norwegian test the waters number one the price of yachts varies Avast Lee an entry-level yacht can cost around $100,000 and the most expensive yacht to date is the Eclipse valued between 700 million and 1.5 billion dollars if you want to be mingling with the high rollers though you'll need to drop at least 20 million dollars on a private yacht like Tiger Woods did when he bought his one hundred and fifty five foot yacht privacy the general rule of thumb is you'll need to put aside ten percent of the cost of the yacht to ensure it's kept running at its optimum each year so in the case of Tiger Woods yacht privacy the additional cost per year would be in the region of two million dollars these costs would vary greatly depending on the purchase price where the yacht is docked how often it's being used the length of the yacht and how old the yacht is as well as what upgrades you've invested in number two registration fees need to be considered these fees can differ depending on the country or state you registering your yacht in international registries will charge the following for a yacht that's 24 meters or 78 feet or less in length in this case a one-off registration fee of five hundred and fifty dollars and then an annual tonnage tax of 750 dollars needs to be paid if the yacht measures more than 24 meters the registration fee is $1500 and the gross tonnage fee of up to 399 tons is seven hundred and fifty dollars four hundred to nine hundred and ninety nine tons is $1,000 and 1012 hundred and fifty dollars and choosing the earth's flag state is a very important decision because that flag state holds the jurisdiction over that specific yacht and in tax obligations privacy regulations liability in international waters and whether the yacht can be used for commercial endeavors number three your yacht needs a place to rest its head where you store your yacht will greatly influence your annual cost if you want people to see you on your pricey yacht then you best choose to to dock your super yacht there if that's out of your budget you could opt for a port to can france which will cost you in the region of 770 dollars a day there are plenty of marina maps available to help you decide the best place to moor your super yacht a less desirable marina would cost roughly $1,200 a month but your additional mooring fees would be incurred when you used your yacht for its intended purposes travelling the globe some marinas fees also consider length and width of your super yacht so expect some fluctuations there as well number four your yacht and your toys will need fuel budgeting your annual fees for fuel can be tricky each yacht is different and the way each yacht is handled is also different if you're cruising at around 8 to 10 knots your fuel usage will be lower than someone going at a faster speed let's assume you have a 60-foot yacht which can hold 1,200 gallons of fuel and the current average price of fuel is roughly two dollars fifty cents a gallon your yacht will cost three thousand dollars to fill out your fuel costs will fluctuate depending on how often you're in the water and also where you're filling up for example the average fuel price in France is roughly four dollars a gallon and in South Africa it's roughly $3 30 cents number five regular wear and tear needs to be factored in like any vehicle purchased regular wear and tear is par for the course to ensure longevity of your yacht regular maintenance is imperative experts estimate that maintenance will be around two percent of the boat's value in the first year and around 7.5% if the yacht is ten years and older your yacht needs to be washed and cleaned every two weeks the bottom of the boat needs to be scrubbed once a month your systems need to be checked regularly every three to six months your yacht should be waxed and every 12 to 24 months you need to repaint the bottom of your boat as the paint layers thin quickly and deteriorate expect to pay around $50 a foot for giving the hull a fresh coat of paint periodic refits are also to be expected you want to look the part all the time so when your yacht is looking a little tired you might want to consider refitting her to the former glory of giving her a much-needed facelift again these expenses can be as high as you're willing to go engines also wear out over time and equipment will need upgrading however there is a way to recoup some of your costs and will tell you how a bit later number six a yacht can't sail all by itself and unless you're able to captain your own ship you'll need a crew on board to do that job for you a 115 foot yacht would need a captain a mate two stewardesses and a chef the salary for each crew member will also fluctuate depending on the size of the yacht the yacht crew salary guide for 2019 recommended the following salaries for yachts measuring between 70 to 100 feet on a yearly basis the captain between sixty-five thousand and one hundred and ten thousand dollars a chef between thirty six thousand two fifty two thousand dollars and a stewardess between thirty to forty eight thousand dollars and if you don't meet your crew full-time you'll need to pay them a daily rate which will be roughly the following on a yacht of the same size the captain between three hundred and three hundred and seventy five dollars a day the chef between 200 to 250 and the stewardess between 175 to 250 dollars a day over and above their salary your crew will need food uniforms transport on board expenses such as linen soap laundry services and so forth if your crew has been with you for a few months you're also liable for their dental and health insurance and full-time crews need four to six weeks of paid holidays each year number seven fine dining is expected on your yacht and these costs can be vastly different if you're hosting parties with canapes and fine champagne your budget will be substantially larger than someone who's making a family tray lasagna with a few beers and a bucket of ice your crew will also need to eat so their dietary requirements need to be taken care of too on a super yacht it's estimated the food beverages and flowers can cost the owner between one and three million dollars a year and if you like to throw the occasional party remember to factor in a deejay decor extra drinks and staff to keep your guests 100% satisfied number eight insurance is non-negotiable your yacht won't be allowed to sail without it the annual insurance fees will be influenced by a few factors including where you keep the yacht the size of the vessel and of course the amenities on board stacks insurance brokerage have been insuring yachts for years and they estimate that a 60-foot yacht insured in the Northeast will cost between 6,000 and 8,000 dollars a year but some will insure the same yacht in Florida and the rate increases between 10,000 and 12,000 dollars and that's only if the yacht is used for private use if you intend to can have on board so you can't rent out your yacht and cram it full of travelers to attempt to make a quick buck the limit is between 12 and 36 passengers regardless of how large the yacht is however it's been noted that when the guest list is higher than 12 it's not technically classified as a charter anymore but rather a passenger ship there's also stiff competition when it comes to chartering
