- How To Charter a Yacht [The Basics]: Kind: captions Language: en-US Hi, this is Candice with Seize the Day Yacht Charters. And today we're talking about how do yacht charters work? I've been a full-time charter professional since 2011. I've booked over a thousand people on charters all over the world from inexpensive trips for college kids to mega yachts for the rich and famous. And of course everything in between. One thing I've found, everybody starts at the same point. How do yacht charters work? What should I expect the trip to be like, what's it going to be like aboard? So that's what we're going to talk about today. Most whole book is charter for seven nights, eight days, and pick a yacht with a captain and a cook. Maybe more, if you have a big group or a big boat, you will complete a preference list in advance of what you'd like to eat and drink. And what you like to do. And the crew will customize a menu and itinerary for you, any sort of food allergies and dietary restrictions can be accommodated. And the itinerary is something you can be as hands-on or hands-off as you want to be frequently, people have one or two spots they really want to visit, and the leave the rest of it for the crew to fill and , to plan out for them. The crew are going to be experts on this sailing destination. They're going to know all of the hotspots to go to and all the hidden coves, and they're going to make any sort of adjustments they need to, along the way based on the weather, it's going to be your boat for the week. If you get somewhere and decide that you really like it, you can stay there for two days. That's happened on trips that I've been on before everybody loved it. And didn't want to leave. So we adjusted our, our itinerary and spent two nights on one Island. So your boat for the week, you can just, as you need to. On the average charter, you're going to unpack your bags once and wake up somewhere new every day. In the morning, when you get up and chef will prepare to fresh breakfast based on your preferences. And then after breakfast and coffee, you'll get sailing. Typically you're going to stay out three to four hours a day. And you might stop somewhere along the way for a snorkel or a swim or a little hike around anIsland. If there's something on the, voyage, you're rarely going to be out of the sight of land. This is called coastal cruising or Island hopping. Often you can see where you're going before you leave. It might just be a speck off in the distance, but you're very rarely out of the sight of land. You're never that far away. Once you get to your destination early afternoon, you have some choices. You can either hang out on the boat and use the water toys. Use the standup paddleboard, a kayak, whatever other features the boat comes with, or we can go with ashore.. In the Caribbean you're going to use a dinghy to get a shore. So a small boat will bring you from out in the water to the shore. And then when you get there, there's going to be beach bars, restaurants, maybe a waterfall hike, or some other natural attraction. In the Mediterranean. You're typically docking each night. So you can walk off and on the boat as you please. There's going to be cobblestone roads, shops to visit. Maybe some fortresses or ruins, it's usually a bit more of a cultural experience and a lot to take in. So, that's how the overview of what it's like to be on a yacht charter for a week. There's obviously a little bit more that goes into it, like picking the right boat and destination, and that's where we come in and help provide some personalized recommendations. It's a bit of a conversation where we get to know you and what you're looking for, and then send you a variety of options that would meet your criteria and then help you evaluate them and narrow it down until you find the perfect yacht. That's it. That's how yacht charters work. In a nutshell. If you have any questions, please don't hesitate to reach out. We'd love to help you plan your next trip. And if you liked this video, give us a like, and follow us on YouTube for more tips on how to plan a charter. Thanks again. Bye.

- Living on a Tiny House Boat for 5 Years Saved His Life – NARROWBOAT STORY: Kind: captions Language: en foreign [Applause] this alternative lifestyle living on a narrowboat off-grid out in the countryside having different scenery every few weeks when you move seeing different Wildlife the Tranquility of it all has saved my life and he and he puts me in a wonderful place and I've learned to live in the moment [Music] I've been living on the boat for five years now and uh it's it's incredible I don't think I could go and live in a house it's just so amazingly different living on a nariable everything slows down and living in such a small space having the least amount of things gives you a lot more freedom you feel a lot Freer you know you've got less to worry about as well for 26 years two children and was working in the pub trade with my wife and we decided to go and live abroad and unfortunately after the first year my wife became very very ill very quickly and passed away and I was left devastated uh you know I was depressed and I had to come back to the UK and I came back with very little money and in a suitcase that was it I ended up couch surfing staying at friends and family's houses and had no home didn't know really where my life was going um but I was really really depressed and nearly ended my life woke up in hospital one day and and that was a bit of an Awakening for me and I ended up getting a job with the canal and River Trust the canal and River trust they look after the canals the 2000 mile Network canals throughout the UK and I became one of their fundraisers and I got chatting and friendly with all the boaters and they were telling me how amazing it was and how it changed their lives and I just thought I gotta do that I need to be on the canal how could I you know but I've got no money I can't afford to to buy an airboat and I went back to a family member was talking about it and said look you know I've got to get out of this depression now I need a home I need somewhere to stay at because I was couch surfing and staying in people's houses that I didn't know my family relative says how much do you want go out buy yourself a boat and and that's what I did and it was only sort of like the second bow that I looked at that you know as soon as I went inside I just thought this is home I could live on this boat it's amazing and so that's how I I got the bow and so grateful for it this lifestyle has saved my life my home is 57 foot long and it was built in 2005. it's called the Liverpool build which were quite common in the days it was quite in a a dirty stay Rusty state but not that bad you know it was just surface rust and but it it needed painting it needed a rub down and never done anything like it in my life before but I managed to rub the whole 57 foot of the boat down um treat the rust put some primer on and repaint it the lovely green and so the interior I've not really done a lot inside um the chap that had it before me it insulated it with foam well he did all the carpentry in here he did all the fitting of the kitchen everything I am a full-time liver board and I'm a continuous Cruiser a continuous Cruiser is a person that doesn't mow their bow in a marina I just literally move every two weeks that's the rules with the canal River trust so for me uh moving the boat it's not a problem I love it because it's like you're on vacation all the time you have two weeks in one beautiful place and then you move on to another place and and the energy is different every time the scene is different to every time living off grid I have solar on the roof I have one solar panel which just provides enough electricity for me to to work on my laptop and and to charge up all my little bits and pieces that I need and the lighting of course and the engine also provides electricity the engine is diesel powered um I would love an electric belt but I haven't got the money to to pay for that so I'm just happy with what I've got I have also um the wood burner which provides me heat through the coldest of winters and it can get very cold in Winter we could get Frozen in sometimes and you really do need that on and it does heat up quite quickly in this bow I have a gas cooker and that the gas is provided by two bottles that I have at the bow of the boat in a container underneath hidden away so I haven't got really a lot of expenses I just pay the canal River truster fee to to live on the canal there's the insurance to pay things like that maintenance of the boat the blacking that needs doing where the boat comes out of the water and it it's basically jet washed down and then re-blacked to protect the whole from corrosion we have to buy fuel for the fire fuel for the engine to work and I have gas for the cooker and that's it that the gas is just for the cooker only but two bottles of gas can last me up to a year it is cheap if you're a continuous Cruiser if you're in a marina like a lot of boaters um they pay a lot more fees and it does cost a lot more and the electricity can be quite expensive um that's why I chose to be a continuous Cruiser keep moving along the canals because it's a lot cheaper way to live and and it's just I just love moving about and just to enjoy that that time at each different Mooring financially I just get by on very little money I look after a family member who's 80 years old I look after them two days a week and I have a small amount of money coming in from donations from my YouTube channel during lockdown my job as a fundraiser with the canal River trust ended and I needed something to do so I created a YouTube channel because I'm a amateur filmmaker self-taught I decided to share all that beautiful footage and and become a vlogger in a way and I have this channel called inspired by nature and it's about just sharing my experiences and putting Positive Vibes out there and showing people how connected with nature can help you there is quite a lot of hard work behind the scenes living on a narrowboat you know you you've got to go and get supplies we're off grid so you know we don't have all continuous water supplied to the bow it's got a tank at the front of the boat so I have to go and fill up the water tank I have to go and get my fuel for the fire and supplies for food and and things like this sometimes I'm moored out in the middle of nowhere maybe 400 yards from the nearest road and I have to go into town get the shop in and bring it back along that toe path in all weathers and so you're trailing that trolley look at it that's what I have is this trolley and um yeah it's it's not all what we call castles and Roses it can be quite difficult at times especially in Winter um when there's lots of snow and sometimes you get Frozen in as well you you can't go anywhere um luckily for us when you're Frozen in the canal River trust that you can't tell you to move on you have to stay where you are um and it and the temperatures can you know inside the boat because the water's cold frozen over at times then the boat can be really cold um and if you if I've gone away for a while and the fire's gone out it could be minus temperatures inside the boat but it doesn't take long to get the fire going and within 2013 minutes or so it starts warming up and it can be very very cozy in Winter inside this but with the the wood burner there um but it's something over the last five years that I've got used to um it seems difficult but you know all these things that like the engine bear you I end up servicing my own engine bay and you get oily and greasy but at the end of the day all the the negatives are outweighed by the positives of living this lifestyle I just enjoy this so much living on the canal it is beautiful and um and like I say not having a lot of things being very simple with with the amount of objects in the bow you know there's not a lot of clutter it's very simple um and I think that's how life needs to be really sometimes we get too attached and and gather too many things I don't have any regrets I'm pleased that I moved on to an hour but I'm just enjoying every moment and that is the the main thing for me and what I've learned to do is live in the moment enjoy now the present time I'm in a good place and I'm lucky as well very lucky to be here thank you subsequent application of a storm ladder don't do this like a cat don't climb here you grasp the rope and put this hand as high as possible and also grasp the Rope yes but that's not all after a jump into the water and a short rest on the life raft an imitation of rescue with a helicopter is to be done an electric winch is like an aircraft well what can I say in a sense to be rescued isn't easier damp conditions in cold conditions is perfect for the development and flaring up of chronic diseases is it really so hard to work on the floating base it's time to check out for myself having dressed in the necessary overalls I'm going to take my BL at the conveyor belt now I am a factory worker you get used to the smell oddly enough pretty quickly but something which is hard to get used to at handlers clean the fish in general in two movements but if I start doing this I can easily cut one of my necessary fingers and secondly responsibility is a pressure for me because I'm cleaning fish for the first time and I understand perfectly that someone will eat the Herring cleaned by me maybe even me and finally for all the apparent Simplicity of these operations I personally do not the catch in the Open Seas lived and worked that is after a 6-hour shift the sail received the same amount of rest time after which the cycle was repeated it is not easy to get used to such a schedule Then you must make sure that your IOBS, that's the paraffin and air tanks, which are always in a surge from below, these have to be pumped up to the pressure. On this particular station, it's 70 lb of air in the tanks because this is what forces the paraffin up to the light itself. While the mess are warming up the burners, you go down and you pump up your air tanks to the pressure. Uh 5 minute job perhaps depending how much it's got to go in upstairs. Again, the sun is probably just gone behind a bank of cloud and time to take the curtains down. Curtains, of course, are put up every day around the lens to stop the sun's rays from striking through it. Reason being, they could quite easily cause a fire. The lens itself acting as a big magnifying glass, you see. So, it's a case of taking the curtains down, folding them up, put them away neatly, and then for this time ready for putting the actual light in. Before the that you actually light the light, you must have your lens revolving. And having done that and your lens revolving back to the actual light itself. And this simply entails turning on your micro valve which lets the paraffin through from the tanks below. Paraffin runs up through the vaporizer which is of course is hot now from the metal edated spirit. The paraffin vaporizes goes up through into the burner head and comes out at the mantle as a vapor. You simply light the paper and up the light comes one big white ball of light. Outside temperature 47. Inside temperature 55, barometer 28.84. Wind direction west. Wind speed 7. During duty on the light every day, 7 days a week, a 24-hour watch is shared between the three keepers. Midnight until 4:00 in the morning, 4:00 a.m. to midday, then 4-hour shifts until midnight. A 56-hour week, none of it counting as overtime. So, this might sound a bit, you know, gumptious or big-headed, but um being on the Bishop Rock, you feel a little a slight cut above the other keepers, but um probably I'm wrong. There's nothing to look at to the probably the ordinary layman, but um to all to myself, it is beautiful. I don't know what I can express in words, but something nice about it. you know, this more so than any other station really because of it of it being so old-fashioned, so antique because the equipment on this station, apart from the engines of course, very very old. - Bad Used Boats to Buy: Kind: captions Language: en your parents might tell you to avoid hanging out with the wrong crowd would a good boat dealership tell you the same thing stick around to find out what used boats to avoid on today's video hi i'm sean from lens cove lessons and boating the used build market is really taking off in north america and that's fueled by everybody wanting to get into the activity of recreational boating with your friends and family and that's a great thing because of that there is a lot less use boat inventory out there so it makes it harder to make sure that you find the right boat for you on today's video we're going to really talk about the four types of used boats you should avoid and why now make sure that if you like this video please throttle that like button and get on board by subscribing for more and hit that notification bell on today's video we're going to cover avoiding boats that have really low hours and haven't been run avoiding boats that have likely sunk steering away from boats that have structural issues and watching out for certain types of power options that can leave you with expensive repairs in the future ultra low hours sounds like a great marketing tagline and definitely lower hours versus really high hours are probably something that you want to consider however if a boat has sat year after year after year perhaps in a customer's garage perhaps in someone's field or for whatever reason it has not been used much that is something that you probably want to avoid find out why and also remember that a boat that sits could have some internal engine damage from not being properly maintained there could be rodents or other animals that have chewed wirings and eventually you're going to have an issue and overall it's just nice to know that a boat was well used and didn't let the customer down probably one of the most dramatic things that can happen to a boat other than catch on fire and burning completely up is sinking problem is a lot of those boats actually come back to life because it is possible to get the water out of all the components to get the water out of the engine and they often fire right back up and work perfectly fine however in the long run you may find that there are more components that fail on a boat that has sunk you could have starter issues alternator issues you could have issues with your gauges your electrical circuits anything that is going to be conducive to getting corrosion on them is a possible failure point for a bow to sunk you're also going to possibly have things in your foam water oil or other contaminants and over time that may really minimize your enjoyment of the boat another big category of boats that on the used market you should avoid are ones with structural issues and it may not be super apparent by an overall viewing of the boat really take your time to look for things like a soft floor and a soft transom when you're inside the boat and you're walking around and you're stepping place to place make sure you try to put your weight in every possible spot on that boat's floor if you feel a soft spot stop and ask some questions it may just mean that there is one little bit of rot in the floor but it may mean that more of the structure below in the stringers are starting to go soft also the transom which is what takes the main thrust of that engine and propels the boat forward could be soft try taking the outboard engine and pushing hard up and down on it to see if there's much flex in the transom if it's an i o or stern drive you're going to want to grab a hold of that lower unit and really lift and drop that to see if there's excess movement in the transom another telltale thing to look for for structural issues are trails of water coming out of fixtures there are lifting eyes on the back of the boat and there's other bolts that go through the transom if you see a water stain that comes down from them you may wonder why is there moisture that's coming out of the boat's core or the transom and running down the outside of the boat or on the inside of the transom another category of used boats to be careful of is ones that have power options that may be hard to repair or are prone to failure the first one we want to talk about is older jet boats any boat that is 20 years or older should be approached cautiously any jet boat that has jet power that is 20 years older should probably be avoided altogether boats that were built prior to 2000 that have jet power were all two-stroke carbureted and definitely had not advanced to the point where they were reliable and we find that the componentry is hard to get so the repairs are expensive or not even possible besides the fact that they're very fuel and efficient and quite loud so i would avoid anything that is jet powered and over 20 years old another engine choice that you're going to want to avoid is anything that is omc powered if it has an omc cobra or an omc stringer you're going to want to avoid that because omc went bankrupt in the early 2000s because of that you have a availability issue with parts you have a technical knowledge issue with not enough mechanics knowing how to fix them and definitely they will be harder to sell on the resale market along the same lines force outboard engines which are no longer in production had a history of older type ignition and fuel delivery systems and overall just less reliable than a lot of other outboard engines lastly volvo made a really revolutionary drive called the xdp drive and it was designed to be very resistant to saltwater contamination because it was built with a composite material however that drive was not robustly built and is very prone to failure and you should avoid it like the plague those are all the types of used boats you should avoid because they are far and few between you won't see them every day they are a little bit like a needle in a haystack but watch out for them particularly in times like we currently have at the time of filming this video when there is very very low inventory what is usually left over has a higher probability of some of these avoidable traps check out the description below for a used boat buying guide and other resources to help you out in your boat buying and check out this link for a video we made for usb buying problems thanks for joining us on today's lens cove lessons and boating make sure you like this video and hit that subscribe button for more content just like this stay safe have fun and we'll see you out on the water

- Who am I? A philosophical inquiry - Amy Adkins: Kind: captions Language: en Throughout the history of mankind, three little words have sent poets to the blank page, philosophers to the Agora, and seekers to the oracles: "Who am I?" From the ancient Greek aphorism inscribed on the Temple of Apollo, "Know thyself," to The Who's rock anthem, "Who Are You?" philosophers, psychologists, academics, scientists, artists, theologians and politicians have all tackled the subject of identity. Their hypotheses are widely varied and lack significant consensus. These are smart, creative people, so what's so hard about coming up with the right answer? One challenge certainly lies with the complex concept of the persistence of identity. Which you is who? The person you are today? Five years ago? Who you'll be in 50 years? And when is "am"? This week? Today? This hour? This second? And which aspect of you is "I"? Are you your physical body? Your thoughts and feelings? Your actions? These murky waters of abstract logic are tricky to navigate, and so it's probably fitting that to demonstrate the complexity, the Greek historian Plutarch used the story of a ship. How are you "I"? As the tale goes, Theseus, the mythical founder King of Athens, single-handedly slayed the evil Minotaur at Crete, then returned home on a ship. To honor this heroic feat, for 1000 years Athenians painstakingly maintained his ship in the harbor, and annually reenacted his voyage. Whenever a part of the ship was worn or damaged, it was replaced with an identical piece of the same material until, at some point, no original parts remained. Plutarch noted the Ship of Theseus was an example of the philosophical paradox revolving around the persistence of identity. How can every single part of something be replaced, yet it still remains the same thing? Let's imagine there are two ships: the ship that Theseus docked in Athens, Ship A, and the ship sailed by the Athenians 1000 years later, Ship B. Very simply, our question is this: does A equal B? Some would say that for 1000 years there has been only one Ship of Theseus, and because the changes made to it happened gradually, it never at any point in time stopped being the legendary ship. Though they have absolutely no parts in common, the two ships are numerically identical, meaning one and the same, so A equals B. However, others could argue that Theseus never set foot on Ship B, and his presence on the ship is an essential qualitative property of the Ship of Theseus. It cannot survive without him. So, though the two ships are numerically identical, they are not qualitatively identical. Thus, A does not equal B. But what happens when we consider this twist? What if, as each piece of the original ship was cast off, somebody collected them all, and rebuilt the entire original ship? When it was finished, undeniably two physical ships would exist: the one that's docked in Athens, and the one in some guy's backyard. Each could lay claim to the title, "The Ship of Theseus," but only would could actually be the real thing. So which one is it, and more importantly, what does this have to do with you? Like the Ship of Theseus, you are a collection of constantly changing parts: your physical body, mind, emotions, circumstances, and even your quirks, always changing, but still in an amazing and sometimes illogical way, you stay the same, too. This is one of the reasons that the question, "Who am I?" is so complex. And in order to answer it, like so many great minds before you, you must be willing to dive into the bottomless ocean of philosophical paradox. Or maybe you could just answer, "I am a legendary hero sailing a powerful ship on an epic journey." That could work, too.

  9. @malmalhi007: The Ship of Theseus is one of philosophy’s oldest puzzles about identity, and it survives because it captures a question that still troubles us today: how can something remain itself when everything about it changes? The story is simple, but the implications reach deep into how we think about people, objects, and even our own lives.

The tale begins with Theseus, the hero of Greek myth. After defeating the Minotaur, he sailed home in a wooden ship that the Athenians preserved as a monument. Over the years, the planks began to rot. To keep the ship seaworthy, the Athenians replaced each plank, one at a time. Eventually, every single piece of wood had been replaced.

At this point, the philosophers asked:
Is it still the same ship?

The riddle sharpens when we imagine a second twist. Suppose someone gathered all the old planks and rebuilt a ship from them. Now we have two vessels:

one with the original shape but new materials
one with the original materials but a new history

Which one is the real Ship of Theseus?

This is where the traditional model of identity begins to strain. It assumes that identity must come from inside a thing — from some hidden core or essence that stays the same while everything else changes. In this view, the ship must have a “true ship‑ness” somewhere within it, and whichever vessel keeps that essence is the real one.

But this assumption is the real problem.

Interpretive Consciousness Theory begins with a different starting point:
no interior is ever seen directly — it is always interpreted from the outside.
We never encounter a hidden essence. We encounter appearances, patterns, and continuities.

Apply that to the Ship of Theseus, and the riddle changes shape.

The ship that remained in Athens — the one that continued to sail, to be repaired, to be recognised, to be honoured — is the one whose pattern continued. Its identity lives in its ongoing appearance and function, not in the survival of any original plank. The ship rebuilt from the old wood shares the materials, but not the continuity. It is a replica, not a continuation.

Identity, in this view, is not something a thing has inside it.
Identity is something a thing does over time.

The Ship of Theseus remains itself because:

its form continues
its role continues
its recognition continues
its story continues

The parts can change. The materials can change. The planks can be replaced one by one. What matters is the continuity of the pattern, not the preservation of an inner essence.

The paradox dissolves because it was built on the wrong assumption. The Ship of Theseus is not a riddle about hidden cores. It is a reminder that identity is interpretive, relational, and visible. The ship is the ship because of the pattern it enacts over time — not because of what lies inside it.

But something changes when we stop trying to make an identity and begin to receive one. When we realize, we were never meant to carry the full weight of defining ourselves. God doesn’t just tell us who we are—He tells us why we are. And when that truth settles in, it doesn’t puff us up or tear us down… it steadies us. Great message.

- Why Billionaires Actually Buy Yachts | Not What you Think: Kind: captions Language: en This boat costs $500 million and it loses roughly $40 million a year just sitting in the water. Fuel for stabilizers, salaries from crew of 30, insurance premiums, docking fees, and premium arenas, routine dry dock inspections, and a relentless upkeep of custom materials exposed assault, sun, and humidity. The math is brutal. The depreciation curve is steep. By traditional financial logic, it makes absolutely no sense. So why do billionaires keep buying them? Why does a demographic that prides itself on ruthless efficiency, datadriven decision-making, and capital allocation consistently pour 9 figures into assets that bleed value from day one? The public answer is simple. Flex culture, vanity, a trophy to park next to the private jet. But you actually study how ultra-igh net worth individuals operate. You quickly realize that narrative collapses under scrutiny. People who build empires do not buy multiund million liabilities just to take photos. They buy them because wealth at a certain threshold stops functioning the way the rest of us understand it. When you cross into nine figure territory, money is no longer a tool for acquisition. It becomes a tool for environmental design. It stops being about adding things to your life and starts being about subtracting everything that drains your time, your focus, and your autonomy. A yacht is not a boat. It is a mobile sovereign zone. It is a controlled ecosystem engineered for privacy, proximity, predictability, and mental clarity. By the end of this breakdown, you will see exactly why the ultra wealthy treat these vessels not as luxury purchases, but as strategic infrastructure. We will walk through the four real drivers behind me acquisition and why each one compounds into something far more valuable than the hull itself. Chapter two, privacy and control. Let us start with the most immediate reality. Modern billionaires live in a surveillance ecosystem that most people cannot even comprehend. Every movement is tracked. Airports log passenger manifests. Ground transportation leaves digital footprints. Hotel bookings are scraped, resold, and weaponized by paparazzi, competitors, or malicious actors. Even walking down a street in a major financial capital means being photographed, geotagged, and dissected in real time. At scale, visibility is not a perk. It is a liability. A mega yacht solves that problem by creating a legally and physically controlled perimeter. Once a vessel crosses into international waters, jurisdictional boundaries shift. Maritime law creates a buffer zone that keeps unwanted attention, unauthorized vessels, and aerial surveillance at a legal and operational distance. But the physical barrier is only half the equation. Modern yachts are engineered as floating secure facilities. Access is governed by biometric verification, encrypted satellite communication systems, and staff will undergo background checks that rival intelligence agency vetting protocols. The guest list is curated down to the individual. No random encounters, no unscheduled meetings, no leaks to the press unless you authorize them. This level of control does not exist on land. Even the most fortified private estates are anchored to a single geographic point surrounded by local infrastructure. Service workers and public airspace that cannot be fully contained. A yacht moves. It repositions. It vanishes from one coordinate and reappears in another. For individuals whose decisions move markets, influence policy or manage generational capital. That kind of operational silence is not indulgence. It is risk mitigation. When you remove unpredictability, you remove stress. When you remove stress, you preserve cognitive bandwidth. And cognitive bandwidth at the highest levels of wealth is the only asset that cannot be purchased. It must be protected. That protection is the first real reason billionaires buy yachts. Not for luxury, for friction removal. Chapter three, access and proximity. But privacy is just the entry fee. The second driver is where the real value multiplies. Proximity. History has always shown that elite networking does not happen in formal boardrooms. It happens in shared isolation. trains in the 19th century, private clubs in the 20th, and now floating platforms in the Mediterranean and Caribbean. The reason is psychological when you remove external noise, shorten the timeline, and place decision makers in a comfortable, self-contained environment. Trust accelerates at an unnatural rate. A traditional business deal requires weeks of due diligence, legal review, scheduling conflicts, and performative negotiation. On a yacht, those friction points dissolve. You are not pitching across a polished table. You are sharing meals, walking decks, observing behavior in unstructured settings. You see how someone handles a delayed itinerary, how they treat staff, how they react when the wifi drops. Those micro signals reveal character faster than any term sheet. And character is what closes generational deals. The charter economy around these vessels operates like a closed loop. Brokers, family office principles, sovereign wealth representatives, venture founders, and cultural influencers are carefully matched through discretion networks. A 5-day itinerary in the Adriatic or the Bahamas is not booked as a vacation. It is booked as a proximity engine. Deals stall in cities for months finalize in 72 hours on water. There's also an unspoken social contract once you step on board. Hierarchy softens. Titles matter less. The environment neutralizes the usual power dynamics of a boardroom or conference stage. You are not their perform. You are there to coexist. That shift unlocks a different kind of influence. Soft power, relationship capital, the kind that opens doors no cold email or broker introduction ever will. Access is not just about who you know. It is about the environment that allows genuine alignment to surface. A yacht manufactures that environment on demand. Chapter 4. The hidden economics. Now, let us address the math. Because if privacy and access were the only drivers, this would still be an unsustainable expense. The truth is the ultra wealthy do not ignore the numbers. They just evaluate them through a completely different framework. A mega yacht is not an investment. It is an operational cost center and family offices modeled exactly that way. Annual operating expenses typically run between 8 and 12% of the vessel initial value. That covers crew salaries which often exceed $2 million annually for a full complement, routine maintenance, classification society inspections, insurance premiums, fuel provisioning and management fees. Depreciation is real. Resale liquidity is thin. Yet these purchases continue because the financial architecture around them is deliberately structured for predictability and liability isolation. Most vessels are flagged in jurisdictions like the Marshall Islands, Cayman Islands, or Malta. They are owned through layered holding companies, often managed by professional yacht management firms that handle compliance, crew employment, and safety certifications. Chartering is sometimes used not as a revenue play, but as a structural tool. A family may charter the vessel back to their own management entity, creating a paper trail that aligns with maritime regulations, optimizes value added tax exposure on fuel and port services, and separates personal use from corporate holding structures. This is not tax evasion. It is legal cost architecture. The goal is never profit. The goal is to convert a volatile, emotionally driven expense into a controlled, predictable line item. In family office modeling, unpredictability is the enemy. Market corrections are expected. Regulatory shifts are anticipated, but personal exposure, reputational risk, and uncontrolled overhead are unacceptable. A yacht structured properly transforms those variables into fixed costs. It becomes a known quantity. And when you operate at scale, a known cost is infinitely preferable to an unknown liability. The math does not justify the purchase on a spreadsheet. It justifies on a balance sheet of risk control and operational stability. Chapter 5, sovereignty and focus. Which brings us to the final and most overlooked driver. Sovereignty, not political, personal at a certain level of success. Wealth stops being about accumulation and starts being about curation. You've already acquired the assets, the experiences, the access. The next question becomes far more fundamental. How do you protect your capacity to think clearly? How do you design an environment where deep strategy, long-term planning, and genuine mental reset can actually occur? Modern life, even for the ultra wealthy, is relentlessly fragmented. Notifications, obligations, travel logistics, public expectations, and the sheer weight of responsibility fracture attention into unusable pieces. A yacht removes a noise. It imposes natural boundaries, no commute, no office politics, no overlapping calendars, just open water, structured routines, and deliberate isolation. It is the ultimate paradox. The most connected people on Earth consistently purchase the most disconnected spaces, not to hide from the world, to step outside of it long enough to see it clearly. Biohacking trends, digital detox retreats, and silent meditation camps all point to the same underlying demand. Focus is the scarcest resource at scale, and focus requires frictionless conditions. When you control your environment, you control your cognition. When you control your cognition, you make better decisions. Better decisions compound into preserved wealth, sustained influence, and long-term legacy. That is the real premium, not the gold fixtures, the helellipads, or the custom interiors. The premium is uninterrupted thought. It is the ability to sit on a deck at dawn with zero external demands and map out the next decade without a single interruption. That mental clarity is worth more than the vessel itself. Outro. So, no. Billionaires do not buy yachts. They buy them because money at scale does not buy more things. It buys fewer interruptions. It buys controlled environments. It buys proximity that accelerates trust. Financial structures that neutralize risk and the rarest commodity of all, unbroken focus. What would you actually do with 48 hours of zero noise, zero schedule, and zero external expectations? Drop your answer in the comments below. And if you want the next deep dive into how family offices actually model wealth preservation, structure legacy assets, and protect generational capital, hit subscribe. I will see you in the next breakdown.

least 18 months before applying for your officer of the watch 3000 certificate of competency 1050 euros time taken five days next up is the rya yacht master offshore theory and practical the theory you can do anytime but there are some prerequisites in terms of experience for the practical so let's say it takes you another six months to gain this experience before you're ready to go for your yacht master cost to you 2 504 euros which includes the vhf license professional practices course and commercial endorsement time taken 14 days and to be clear this is for the motor yacht master which is more expensive than the sale yacht master because as part of the practical course you've got to pay for the fuel in the motor yacht now at this point you could already become a superior captain up to vessels of 200 gross tons in volume but if this is as far as you want to go may i suggest that you at least go ahead and do the next ticket or qualification because it will just help you to be a much better master all round you're going to need to do two more courses that's the gm dss global maritime distress and safety system it's a radio course you've got the roc and the goc the restricted operators certificate and the general operator certificate i suggest you skip the roc go straight to the goc because this gives you unlimited range cost to you 1 924 euros time taken 10 days the other course is helm operational there are two helm courses one is one is management you can't skip the and go straight to management so operational first cost to you 799 euros time taken three days in addition to doing these courses you're gonna need to hold your yacht master for at least six months to take the mca master 200 exam now this exam is not mandatory but i've done it and i highly recommend it both if you only want to skip vessels up to 200 tons because in this instance it sharpens your skills and your knowledge but also if you want to carry on up the ranks it's a great stepping stone and it gets you into the mca framework and way of thinking cost to you 245 euros having passed that exam you're going to be in a great place to carry on working your way up the ranks radar cost to you three thousand four hundred and ten euros time taken 15 days now it's just a case of continuing to build your sign on time and see time as you build up to the required amount for the oow 3000 exam it's now going to be about time that you renew your eng-1 they're only valid for two years so that's going to be another 120 euros once you've finally reached that 36 months of sign on year or 95 pounds to 450 pounds per foot in croatia mooring fees are substantially higher with rates ranging from 435 pounds to 1050 pounds per meter per year or 145 pounds to 350 pounds per foot for a high season contract greece's annual mooring fees are relatively lower falling between 225 pounds and 525 pounds per meter per year or between 75 pounds and 175 pounds per foot but monaco is one of the most expensive destinations for mooring fees with a typical rate of 1500 per meter per year so when choosing a location in the mediterranean be sure to factor in the cost of mooring fees into your from 12 euros to 50 euros per foot of boat length per year popular locations such as los angeles and florida have higher mooring fees starting at around 120 euros per foot and reaching up to 240 euros per foot for transient mooring the docking fees are not based on the boat's length and are typically 30 euros so when planning your next sailing adventure be sure to factor in mooring fees to help your budget for your trip number five yacht maintenance schedule everything on your yacht that needs to be monitored and taken care of constantly hull and bottom clean and polish regularly to prevent any signs of structural damage repair as necessary rig and sails inspect regularly for wear and tear repair or replace as necessary engine and machinery consult manufacturers manuals for service clean and polish all surfaces regularly check for any signs of damage or wear and tear repair or replace as necessary exterior wash and wax the hull and deck regularly polish brightwork in stainless steel fixtures check for any signs of structural damage general perform a thorough inspection of the yacht on a regular basis looking for any signs of damage or wear and tear and repair and replace as necessary it is important to keep the exterior of your yard clean and free of streaks dust and bird droppings you can remove canvas covers and wipe down upholstery to keep it clean metal should be cleaned and polished and woodwork scrubbed a thorough exterior cleaning will cost on average two to three dollars per foot yacht detail companies can also clean the interior which may involve dusting regular basis is important a smart clean hull is crucial to long-term care and efficient performance of the yacht typically this involves a diver cleaning the hull from the water line and below to They can sit in their office with no natural light obviously, but they're just sitting there making sure all the systems are okay. And obviously on a yacht like this, there are lots of systems to manage. And it has this hybrid diesel electric system. So you don't actually have main engines. We got no shaft line because it uses pods, five variable speed generators that can be run in any sequence depending on what the boat's doing when it comes to propulsion or domestic supply. You can see one of the pods there. Of course, there's another one on the other side. And you have a traditional bow thruster as well. But think of this like Volvo Penta IPS pod drives. Apart from the differences IPS turns like that, these can spin all the way around making Ken show extremely maneuverable to 75 m yacht. And there are other benefits as well. If you want to move along quietly, say in the morning when guest is sleeping, you can move this thing running just one generator rather than firing up a pair of big main engines. And also the pods are right back in the boat as well. It's a really really clever system. What Kensho demonstrates is what's feasible in the world of superyachts. You can start with a concept that came from a sketch on a piece of paper, a glimmer of an idea that develops into something that is truly gamechanging. This is genuinely unique. There's even a thing called a illiquidity premium. So they are illiquid. There's no way to pretend they're not illiquid. And if somebody puts a wrapper, there's no wrapper that you can put around private markets to make them liquid without diminishing their returns. There are about 8 million privately held companies in America. There are 4000 publicly traded So that's 20 times as many investment And they can only private equity investors can buy them. And she has a new book out called The Age Rebellion Supercharge the Second Half of Your Life. Joining us right now here in studio two is Lindsey Simpson, founder and CEO of 55 redefined, an enterprise age intelligence and tech solutions platform. Leslie, great to have you here. Thank you very much. Let's talk about, uh, the aging of our population. We've talked about this a lot over the last few years. Uh, obviously with the advancements in medical sciences, people are living longer. They're also living healthier, which of course, has extended, uh, their, uh, their lifetime in the workforce. And you sort of posit this idea that companies haven't quite caught up with that or embraced it. What's happening? Yeah, 100%. We have added 30 healthy years to life expectancy in the last century alone. Yet we're still designing our companies and our workforce strategies as if it was a century ago where we still got the same retirement age and we're still checking people out, really in their 50s and 60s in terms of the value. Now we have a predominant over 50s population and they are the fastest growing element of our workforce and of our consumer base. It's really important that companies lean in to see that as a growth lever, an opportunity rather than an economic drag. But but historically, we know I mean, we'll just be delicate about that. But a lot of companies, they look at older workers, they see them as a drag. Usually they're higher paid, even though they are more experience. And the idea is they feel like, okay, we can get someone half their age to do the same job for presumably a lot less There's also the issue of benefits. Uh, obviously, aging brings a lot of other challenges with people having to take care of elderly family members, things like that that you don't necessarily have to deal with with the younger workforce. So how do you sort of square that circle to show that there is an advantage of keeping these people around? So you're talking about their ROI return on investment. I talk about ROI, return on experience. And what we see is when you look beyond the paycheck, um, what we see is that an over 50 worker is 200% less likely to take a day of work sick than a colleague under 30. We see that they are five times less likely to change jobs in the first 18 months, which brings down attrition and hiring costs massively. When you look at large global organizations and when it comes to customer outcomes and revenue generation, they are disproportionately at their worst. So when we start to look at the real data picture that's happening in organizations, we see that that ROI, that return on experience pays more dividends than the paycheck. We'll talk to us about what companies are actually doing. First of all, whether or not this is even recognized as a problem, this age problem, and if so, if it is acknowledged, I mean, what corporations are actually how they're addressing it. Well, we work with over 300 global corporates, and they will include names that you will recognize like Amazon and AXA in Asia. And what we're seeing is that they have recognized the fact that they are missing the missed middle of their workforce. They've been focusing their talent strategies on early careers and youth, but with the lowest birth rates on record, we're seeing a diminishing talent pool from that line. Whereas if we start to lean into our midlife and our mid-career populations, then we unlock the biggest opportunity for our businesses, and that's productive growth. And if we can then extend careers by five, ten years, which is invariably what we do with the large organizations we work with. We see that retirement savings gap close, which is essential for our economies, is essential for our Our talent and skills crisis that we have across many industries. But it also is essential to us as humans who don't want to be checked out of life and work, you know, halfway through our time. Well, as you said, I mean you work with hundreds of multinationals. Uh, you say that you've created the world's first age intelligence platform. I want to understand a little bit more what that is, what age intelligence means, what your platform actually does. Well, it does two things. It's called me redefined and it's game changing actually. So for the individual they go into a bit like this, a movie set scenario, whether the lead actor in hero in the story of their life, but the script is only halfway written because they're only halfway through the life. So we'll take them up to writing their 100th birthday speech a bit like Sir David Attenborough this week, and we take them then right back to today. And what does that mean for the next five years, ten years and career, life, work, finances. But for the employer, they get the holy grail, which is age intelligence. When are people thinking of retiring? You know, you can't in the US ask somebody, when are you going to retire? Because it's an illegal question, but we can aggregate and anonymize that data and start to see the risks and the patterns. And where do we lean in?
